title: Why AAA Studios Are Eyeing Browser Distribution
description: Cloud gaming stumbles, but the browser as a game platform is gaining traction — for reasons the industry didn't expect.
content:
## The Cloud Gaming Graveyard

On January 18, 2023, **Google Stadia officially shut down**. The service, launched in November 2019 with enormous fanfare and Google's characteristic confidence, had promised to be the future of gaming: AAA titles streaming from Google's data centers to any device with a Chrome browser.

Google invested heavily — acquiring studios, signing exclusivity deals, building custom server hardware. And then, less than three and a half years later, they killed it. Google refunded all hardware and game purchases, a move that cost them hundreds of millions and served as a final, expensive acknowledgment that the product hadn't found a market.

Stadia's failure wasn't primarily technical. The streaming technology worked reasonably well on fast connections. The failure was **product-market fit**: players didn't want to buy games on a platform they didn't trust Google to maintain (a justified concern, as it turned out), the latency was noticeable enough to frustrate core gamers, and the value proposition over owning a console or PC was unclear.

But Stadia's death didn't kill cloud gaming. It just clarified who the real players are.

## The Survivors

**Xbox Cloud Gaming (xCloud)** has emerged as the most credible cloud gaming platform, largely because Microsoft made a strategic decision that Google didn't: they bundled it into an existing subscription. Xbox Game Pass Ultimate subscribers get cloud gaming as a perk, not a standalone purchase. This means players aren't betting on a new platform — they're getting a bonus feature on a service they already pay for.

xCloud now supports hundreds of titles playable via browser, and Microsoft has been steadily improving stream quality and reducing latency. In 2024, they began testing cloud gaming on Samsung smart TVs and Amazon Fire TV devices, expanding access beyond traditional gaming hardware.

**NVIDIA GeForce Now** has carved out a different niche: it lets players stream games they already own on Steam, Epic, and other PC storefronts. This solves the library problem that plagued Stadia — you're not rebuying games on a new platform. GeForce Now's highest tier offers RTX 4080 equivalent performance, making it a viable option for players who want to play graphically demanding games without a $2,000 GPU.

**Netflix** entered the cloud gaming conversation in 2024, announcing its push into cloud-streamed games accessible through its app and website. Netflix's approach leverages its massive subscriber base and frictionless access model — the same logic that makes Xbox's bundling strategy work.

The pattern is clear: cloud gaming survives not as a standalone product, but as a feature of a larger ecosystem.

## The Latency Problem Nobody Can Solve

Here's the fundamental issue with cloud gaming that no amount of server infrastructure can fix: **physics**.

When you stream a game from a data center, every input must travel from your device to the server, the server must process a frame, and the rendered frame must travel back. Even at the speed of light, a round trip from New York to a data center in Virginia takes roughly 10 milliseconds — and real-world networking adds processing delays, routing hops, and jitter that push total input latency to 40-80 milliseconds on a good connection.

For some games, this is acceptable. Turn-based strategy games, narrative adventures, and slower-paced RPGs can tolerate 60ms of latency without degraded experience. For others — fighting games, rhythm games, competitive shooters, platformers — it's a dealbreaker. Professional fighting game players can detect latency as low as 3-4 frames (50-67ms at 60fps), and competitive shooters require sub-30ms response times for responsive aiming.

This isn't a problem that faster internet or closer data centers will fully solve. Light has a speed limit, and real-world networks have overhead. Cloud gaming will always have a latency floor that local execution doesn't.

## The Browser-Native Alternative

This is where our perspective comes in, and it's why we think AAA studios should be paying attention to browser-native games, not just cloud streaming.

**A browser-native game runs locally.** The code downloads to the player's device — JavaScript, WebAssembly, assets — and executes on their hardware. Input latency is identical to a native application: the frame loop reads input, updates state, and renders in the same process. There's no server round-trip for gameplay.

The advantages are significant:

### Zero latency penalty
The game runs on the player's device. Input response is limited only by the device's processing speed and display refresh rate, not by network conditions.

### No server infrastructure costs for single-player
Cloud gaming requires expensive GPU servers running 24/7. A browser-native game requires only a web server to deliver static files (HTML, JS, WASM, assets), which can be served from a CDN for pennies per gigabyte.

### Works offline
Progressive Web App (PWA) technologies allow browser games to cache assets and run without an internet connection. Cloud-streamed games require a constant, high-bandwidth connection.

### Scales with player hardware
As devices get faster — better mobile GPUs, more RAM, more CPU cores — browser-native games automatically benefit. Cloud games are limited by the server hardware the provider allocates, and upgrades require capital expenditure.

### Universal access
Any device with a modern browser can play. No app store approval. No platform-specific builds. No compatibility matrices. One codebase, every platform.

## Why AAA Studios Are Paying Attention

Several trends are converging:

**Distribution costs are rising.** Steam takes 30%. Apple takes 30%. Google takes 30% (reduced to 15% for the first million in some cases). Console licensing fees add more. For a AAA studio shipping a $70 game, platform fees represent an enormous cost. A browser game distributed through the studio's own website pays zero platform fees.

**Web technology is maturing.** WebGPU, WebAssembly, WebTransport, WebCodecs — the browser is gaining capabilities that were previously exclusive to native platforms. The gap between what you can build in a browser and what you can build natively is shrinking with every Chrome release.

**Player behavior is shifting.** Younger players are accustomed to playing games in browsers (Roblox's web client), on phones (mobile-first markets), and through links shared in social platforms. The expectation that a "real game" requires a 100GB download and a $500 console is generational, and it's fading.

**Instant play is a competitive advantage.** When Fortnite launched on iOS via xCloud after being banned from the App Store, players could access it through a browser bookmark. The experience wasn't as polished as a native app, but the *access* was instant. That matters.

## The Hybrid Future

We don't think cloud gaming and browser-native gaming are competitors. They're complementary approaches to the same goal: **getting games to players with minimal friction**.

Cloud gaming is the right approach for graphically demanding AAA titles that require hardware players don't own. If you want to play a game that needs an RTX 4080 and you have a Chromebook, cloud streaming is the only option.

Browser-native gaming is the right approach for games designed for the web — games that are built to run on the hardware people actually have, that prioritize accessibility and instant access, and that don't need photorealistic rendering to deliver compelling experiences.

At RMH Studios, we've bet on browser-native. Our games — party games, rhythm games, puzzle games, narrative games, competitive typing — run on any device with a browser. They load in seconds. They don't buffer. They don't lag because someone between you and a data center is downloading a movie.

When AAA studios look at the browser, they see what we've seen for years: a distribution channel with zero platform fees, universal reach, and instant access. The tools to build great games on it already exist. The audience is already there. The only thing left is the ambition to build for it.

title: Accessibility in Browser Game UI Design
description: Building games that everyone can play isn't charity — it's craft. Here's how we approach accessibility.
content:
## 60+ Accessibility Options in a AAA Game

When Naughty Dog shipped **The Last of Us Part II** in June 2020, it included over 60 accessibility options — a number that stunned the industry. Players could enable high-contrast mode, text-to-speech for all UI elements, audio cues for navigation, remappable controls, auto-aim with granular adjustment, and dozens of other features that made the game playable by people with a wide range of physical, visual, auditory, and cognitive abilities.

The game was praised not just by accessibility advocates but by mainstream reviewers and players. It won multiple accessibility awards and set a new benchmark that other studios have been measured against ever since.

The lesson was clear: **accessibility isn't a niche feature — it's a mark of quality.** A game that more people can play is a better game, period.

We took that lesson seriously. As a browser game studio, we have both unique challenges and unique advantages when it comes to accessibility. This post is about how we think about it and what we actually do.

## The Legal and Ethical Landscape

Accessibility in digital products isn't just a best practice — it's increasingly a legal requirement.

The **21st Century Communications and Video Accessibility Act (CVAA)**, signed into law in 2010 in the United States, requires that communications functionality in games and software be accessible to people with disabilities. While the CVAA's gaming provisions (which took effect in 2019 after an extended waiver) focus primarily on communication features — voice chat, text chat, game menus related to communication — the law signals a clear regulatory direction.

The **Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1**, published by the W3C in 2018, provide the technical standard for web accessibility. WCAG defines four principles — perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust — and three conformance levels: A, AA, and AAA. For web-based applications, WCAG 2.1 AA is widely considered the minimum standard, and it's the standard that courts have referenced in accessibility lawsuits against web companies.

For us, this legal landscape isn't a threat — it's a framework. WCAG gives us concrete, testable criteria for accessibility. The CVAA gives us guidance on where the floor is. And our own values tell us to aim well above the floor.

## Browser Games: The Unique Advantages

Building games for the browser gives us accessibility advantages that native game developers don't have:

### The platform does a lot of the work

Browsers are the most accessible application runtime in existence. Screen readers (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver) are built to interact with web content. The HTML semantics system — headings, landmarks, ARIA roles, form labels — provides a rich vocabulary for communicating UI structure to assistive technology.

When we build a game menu as semantic HTML rather than a canvas-rendered custom UI, screen readers can navigate it automatically. When we use proper `<button>` elements instead of `<div onClick>` handlers, keyboard users get focus management for free. The web platform has spent decades solving these problems.

### No installation barrier

A browser game requires no app store download, no hardware driver installation, no compatibility check. If your device has a browser, you can play. This is itself an accessibility feature — it removes the financial and technical barriers that prevent many people from accessing native games.

### CSS handles responsive design

With CSS media queries, `prefers-reduced-motion`, `prefers-color-scheme`, `prefers-contrast`, and viewport-relative units, we can adapt our games to a huge range of display configurations without requiring the player to find and adjust an in-game settings menu.

## What We Actually Implement

Here are the specific accessibility features we build into our games:

### Keyboard Navigation

Every interactive element in every RMH game is reachable and operable via keyboard. Tab order follows logical reading order. Focus indicators are visible and high-contrast. Game actions can be triggered with Enter, Space, or custom keybindings.

For games with real-time input (rhythm games like Slice It!, reflex-based minigames in RMHbox), we provide keyboard alternatives to click/touch targets and configurable key bindings so players can use whatever layout works for their abilities.

### Color and Contrast

We follow WCAG 2.1 AA contrast ratios as a minimum: **4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text and UI components**. We test our color palettes with tools like the Colour Contrast Analyser and browser-based simulators for protanopia, deuteranopia, and tritanopia.

Critically, we **never rely on color alone to convey information**. In a game like Laundry Sort, where items need to be sorted by category, we use shape, pattern, and label in addition to color. In Signal Forge, card types are distinguished by icon and border style, not just color.

### Screen Reader Support

Our game menus, lobbies, leaderboards, and non-gameplay UI are built with semantic HTML and ARIA attributes. We use `aria-live` regions to announce state changes — "Round 3 of 5," "Your score: 1,200," "Timer: 30 seconds remaining" — so screen reader users have real-time awareness of game state.

Real-time gameplay is harder to make screen-reader accessible, and we're honest about the fact that some of our games — particularly visually intensive ones like Neon Driftway and Echoes of the Spire — have core mechanics that are fundamentally visual. We're researching sonification and haptic alternatives, but we won't pretend we've solved this for every game.

### Reduced Motion

We respect the `prefers-reduced-motion` CSS media query. When a player's operating system indicates a preference for reduced motion, we:

- Disable parallax scrolling and decorative animations
- Replace animated transitions with instant state changes
- Reduce particle effects and screen shake
- Provide static alternatives to animated UI elements

This isn't just about vestibular disorders — it also improves battery life, reduces distraction, and makes the experience more comfortable for anyone who finds excessive motion unpleasant.

### Text and Font Options

We support browser-level text scaling. Our UI layouts are built with relative units (`rem`, `em`, `%`) so that increasing the browser's default font size doesn't break layouts. We avoid text embedded in images, and we provide sufficient line spacing and paragraph width to support comfortable reading.

## The Ongoing Work

Accessibility is not a feature you ship once and check off a list. It's a **continuous practice** that requires testing, feedback, and iteration.

We maintain an accessibility checklist that we run against every new game and every major update. We test with keyboard-only navigation, screen readers (VoiceOver on macOS, NVDA on Windows), and simulated vision conditions. We actively seek feedback from players with disabilities, because automated testing catches conformance issues but misses usability problems.

We also recognize our limitations. We're a small studio. We don't have a dedicated accessibility team with the resources of Naughty Dog or Microsoft. Some of our older games have accessibility gaps that we're working to close. We'd rather be honest about the current state and committed to improvement than claim we've solved accessibility completely.

## Why This Matters for the Industry

The browser game space — and the indie game space more broadly — has historically treated accessibility as an afterthought. The assumption is that accessibility is expensive, time-consuming, and relevant only to a small percentage of players.

All three assumptions are wrong. **The World Health Organization estimates that 1.3 billion people — roughly 16% of the global population — experience significant disability.** When you build an inaccessible game, you're telling 16% of your potential audience that they don't matter.

The good news is that the web platform makes accessibility easier than any other game distribution platform. The tools exist. The standards exist. The only thing missing is the will.

We're committed to doing this work, and we'll keep sharing what we learn.

title: The Achievement System: Auditing and Visualizing Progress
description: A detailed look at the UI redesign process for The Achievement System: Auditing and Visualizing Progress.
content:
UI design is never finished. It evolves. For The Achievement System: Auditing and Visualizing Progress, our goal was to bridge the gap between functionality and aesthetic brilliance. In this post, we'll dive deep into the specific challenges we faced and the solutions we implemented.

## The Objective

The primary goal of the The Achievement System: Auditing and Visualizing Progress redesign was to enhance user engagement while maintaining technical efficiency. We wanted users to feel a sense of fluid interaction, where every click or hover provided meaningful feedback. This required a fundamental rethink of our existing components and spiritual alignment with the overall rmhstudios brand.

We started by auditing our current implementation. What was working? What felt clunky? What was redundant? By stripping back the unnecessary elements, we were able to focus on the core user journey. For The Achievement System: Auditing and Visualizing Progress, this meant focusing on clarity and speed.

## Iterative Design Process

Design at rmhstudios is iterative. We don't just build once and ship. We prototype, test, and refine. For this particular update, we went through three major stages of development:

1.  **Conceptual Wireframing**: Mapping out the raw data flow and user interaction points. We ignored visuals at this stage to focus purely on UX.
2.  **Visual Prototyping**: Applying our signature neon aesthetic. This involved heavy use of CSS variables to ensure consistency across light and dark modes.
3.  **Technical Integration**: Merging the design with our Next.js and Tailwind architecture. Performance was a key metric here—we couldn't let visual polish compromise load times.

One specific challenge was handling responsive layout shifts. As screens get smaller, information density needs to be managed carefully. We implemented custom media queries and flexible grid systems to ensure that The Achievement System: Auditing and Visualizing Progress looks stunning on everything from a widescreen monitor to a handheld device.

## Key Technical Details

From a technical perspective, we relied heavily on modern CSS features. We used `clamp()` for fluid typography and `grid-template-areas` for complex layouts. This allowed us to write cleaner code that is easier to maintain. We also leaned into the power of Framer Motion for micro-animations. These subtle movements—like a button gently pulsing or a card sliding into view—add a layer of polish that makes the web feel alive.

We also prioritized accessibility. Every redesign is an opportunity to improve. We audited our color contrast ratios and ensured that all interactive elements are keyboard-navigable. A beautiful UI is useless if it's not inclusive.

## The Impact

Since implementing these changes, we've seen a noticeable improvement in user retention and satisfaction. The feedback has been overwhelmingly positive. Users appreciate the cleaner aesthetic and the more intuitive navigation. It's a testament to the idea that small, thoughtful changes can have a massive impact on the overall experience.

We are particularly proud of the performance gains. By optimizing our asset delivery and refining our component logic, we've managed to reduce the cumulative layout shift (CLS) and improve the largest contentful paint (LCP) across the site.

## Looking Forward

This is just the beginning. As we move closer to the launch of Project Vega and further updates to our existing games, we'll continue to push the boundaries of what's possible in web-based game UI. We're already experimenting with more advanced shader effects and interactive 3D elements that sit directly in the DOM.

The journey of design is continuous. We're always learning, always growing, and always looking for ways to make rmhstudios.com the best it can be. Stay tuned for more updates as we continue to refine our vision.

## Conclusion

The redesign of The Achievement System: Auditing and Visualizing Progress represents a significant milestone in our design evolution. It's not just about making things look "pretty"; it's about creating an experience that is functional, accessible, and delightful. We hope this deep dive into our process provides some insight into how we approach UI at rmhstudios.

What are your thoughts on this new direction? Do you have suggestions for future redesigns? We'd love to hear from you in the community Discord or via our feedback forms. Your input helps shape the future of this studio.

Thanks for reading, and keep on creating!

title: Continued Development: Doors & Navigation
description: Implementing seamless door transitions and room navigation logic.
content:
Day 2 of development on **Satan's Library**. After nailing the camera system yesterday, today was all about transitions. ## The Door Problem In survival horror, doors are more than just portals; they are loading screens, pacing mechanisms, and sources of tension. We wanted to keep that tension without the loading times.
[Placeholder: Door Opening Animation]
### Implementation Details We're using a seamless streaming tech that pre-loads the adjacent room as you approach the door handle. - **Interaction**: You have to physically rotate the analog stick (or mouse) to turn the handle, adding to the immersion. - **Creak Physics**: The speed of the door opening affects the sound it makes using our custom audio engine. Tomorrow we start working on the **Succubi AI** pathfinding. Wish us luck.

title: An Honest Take on AI in Game Development
description: AI is transforming game development — but not in the ways the hype suggests. Here's where we draw the line.
content:
## The Hype Curve Is Steep

According to **Gartner's 2024 Hype Cycle for Emerging Technologies**, generative AI had crested the "Peak of Inflated Expectations" and was beginning its slide into the "Trough of Disillusionment." If you've been following AI discourse in the game industry, that trajectory feels about right.

In 2023, every conference keynote promised that AI would revolutionize game development. Procedural worlds would generate themselves. NPCs would hold meaningful conversations. Asset pipelines would shrink from months to hours. By mid-2024, the conversation had grown considerably more nuanced — not because AI had failed, but because the gap between demos and production-ready tools had become impossible to ignore.

We use AI tools at RMH Studios. We're also deeply skeptical of AI hype. Those two positions are not contradictory. In fact, we think holding both is the only honest posture for a game studio in 2025.

## Where AI Actually Helps Us

### Code Assistance

**GitHub Copilot** has become a genuine productivity tool for our development workflow. We use it for boilerplate generation, test scaffolding, API integration patterns, and the kind of repetitive code that every developer writes but nobody enjoys writing. It's particularly effective for our stack — Next.js, React, TypeScript, WebSocket handlers — because these are well-represented in its training data.

The Game Developers Conference (GDC) 2024 survey found that approximately 31% of game developers reported using AI tools in their workflow, with code assistance being the most common application. That tracks with our experience. Copilot doesn't write our game logic — the creative, architectural decisions that define what a game feels like. But it accelerates the implementation of those decisions.

### Prototyping and Ideation

We use large language models as brainstorming partners. When designing minigames for RMHbox, we'll describe a mechanic and ask for variations, edge cases, or potential balance issues. The output isn't directly usable — it needs heavy filtering by someone who actually understands game design — but it's useful as a creativity prompt. Think of it as rubber-duck debugging for design.

### Documentation and Content

Writing API documentation, help text, patch notes, and internal wikis is work that benefits from AI drafting. We always review and revise, but starting from a draft rather than a blank page saves meaningful time.

## Where We Draw the Line

### We Don't Use AI to Replace Artists

This is the line, and we want to be unambiguous about it.

The past two years have seen a wave of AI image generators — Midjourney, Stable Diffusion, DALL-E — marketed as replacements for concept artists, illustrators, and visual designers. Some studios have quietly replaced art teams with AI pipelines. Others have been less quiet about it, and the backlash has been severe.

The concerns are not abstract. The **SAG-AFTRA strike** of 2023, which lasted 118 days and was the longest actors' strike in SAG-AFTRA's history, centered in part on AI protections — specifically, the right of performers to control how their likeness and voice are used by AI systems. While the strike focused on film and television, the precedent reverberates through every creative industry, including games.

In the visual arts community, concept artists have reported layoffs explicitly tied to studios adopting AI image generation. A widely shared 2024 survey by the Concept Art Association found that a significant majority of respondents had experienced reduced work opportunities that they attributed at least partially to AI tools.

The ethical problems are compounded by the legal ones. Most AI image generators were trained on datasets scraped from the internet without artist consent — a practice that is the subject of multiple ongoing lawsuits, including cases brought by artists against Stability AI and Midjourney.

Our position: **the people who create art deserve to be compensated for their work, credited for their contributions, and in control of how their creations are used.** We use AI for code assistance and text drafting, where the tools are trained on data with clearer licensing (Copilot is trained on public repositories, many with open-source licenses). We do not use AI image generators for game assets.

### We Don't Ship AI-Generated Game Content to Players

Some studios have experimented with AI-generated dialogue, quests, and narrative content. The results, so far, have been mediocre at best. AI-generated text is fluent but shallow — it lacks the intentionality, the surprise, the *craft* that makes great game writing memorable.

When we write dialogue for Echoes of the Spire or flavor text for Signal Forge, a human being writes it. They make choices about tone, rhythm, subtext, and emotional pacing that reflect a creative vision. We're not willing to outsource that to a statistical model, no matter how convincing the output appears.

## The Nuance the Industry Needs

The AI conversation in gaming has become polarized. On one side: breathless techno-optimism, treating every AI capability as an inevitable and unqualified good. On the other: total rejection, treating any use of AI tools as a betrayal of creative values.

We think both positions are wrong.

AI code assistants are **tools**, like IDEs, debuggers, and linters before them. They make developers faster at tasks that were already within their capabilities. That's unambiguously useful.

AI art generators raise **ethical concerns** about consent, compensation, and the displacement of creative workers. Those concerns deserve serious engagement, not dismissal.

AI content generation is **immature** for production game content. It might improve. When it does, the conversation about how to use it responsibly will still matter.

## What We'd Like to See

- **Industry standards for AI disclosure.** Players deserve to know when a game's content — art, writing, music, voice acting — was generated by AI. Not because AI content is inherently bad, but because informed consumers make better choices.

- **Licensing clarity for training data.** The legal ambiguity around AI training datasets needs resolution. Studios should be able to clearly state that their AI tools were trained on properly licensed data.

- **Protection for creative workers.** Game development employs hundreds of thousands of artists, writers, composers, and voice actors. The industry has a responsibility to ensure that productivity gains from AI don't come at the cost of these people's livelihoods.

- **Honest marketing.** When a studio demonstrates an "AI-powered feature," they should be clear about what the AI actually does, what its limitations are, and what human oversight is involved. The demo-to-reality gap in AI marketing is corrosive to trust.

## Our Commitment

We'll keep using AI tools where they make us more productive without compromising our values. We'll keep not using them where the ethical ground is uncertain. And we'll keep being transparent about which is which.

AI is a powerful set of technologies. It's not magic, it's not going to replace game developers, and it's not going to make great games by itself. The studios that navigate this moment well will be the ones that use AI honestly — as a tool in service of human creativity, not a replacement for it.

That's where we stand. We'll update you if our thinking evolves, because honesty means being willing to change your mind when the evidence warrants it.

title: Boss Mechanics 101: Scaling the Threat
description: How we designed the periodic boss encounters to keep players on their toes.
content:
# The Titan Approaches

In *Echoes*, time is your greatest enemy. Every 2 minutes, a Boss entity spawns to test your build's viability.

## Scaling Difficulty
We don't just increase health. Bosses in the late game possess:
- **Bullet Novas**: 360-degree projectile bursts that force movement.
* **Aggressive Dashing**: High-speed maneuvers that can cut off escape routes.
* **Quadratic Stat Growth**: HP scales faster than player damage, making late-game encounters a genuine struggle for survival.

## Phase State Machine
Bosses cycle through three primary states:
1. **Chasing**: standard movement toward the player.
2. **Charging**: preparing for a high-damage dash.
3. **Bursting**: unleashing a hail of projectiles.

Survive the boss, and the rewards (massive XP drops) will push you toward the next level of evolution.

title: The Browser Advantage in 2026
description: WebGPU, WASM, and modern engines are making the browser a legitimate gaming platform — and we're positioned to lead.
content:
## The Platform Nobody Expected

Five years ago, if you told a game developer that the browser would be a serious gaming platform in 2026, they'd have laughed. Browsers were for websites, web apps, and maybe some casual Flash-successor games. The idea of shipping a competitive, visually rich, multiplayer game through a browser tab felt naive.

Nobody's laughing now.

The convergence of three technologies — **WebGPU, WebAssembly, and modern JavaScript engines** — has quietly transformed the browser from a toy platform into a legitimate contender for game distribution. And the non-technical advantages of the browser — instant access, zero installs, cross-platform by default — haven't changed. They've just become more powerful as the technical limitations fall away.

## The Technology Stack in 2026

### WebGPU: Shipping and Maturing

WebGPU has been available in Chrome since April 2023, and the past two-plus years have seen it mature significantly. Safari's implementation has progressed through multiple releases, and Firefox's Rust-based `wgpu` implementation is nearing stable readiness.

The practical impact: as of early 2026, WebGPU is accessible to the vast majority of desktop browser users. Chrome's roughly **65% desktop market share** (per StatCounter's December 2025 data) means most users already have WebGPU support. Add Safari's approximately **18% share** with its advancing implementation, and you're covering the dominant browsers.

For game developers, this means compute shaders, modern render pipelines, and GPU-driven rendering are no longer theoretical — they're deployable. The gap between "what a browser can render" and "what a native application can render" has narrowed dramatically.

### WebAssembly: Near-Native Speed

WebAssembly has matured beyond its initial promise. The WASM specification now includes features like SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data), which enables vectorized math operations critical for physics, audio processing, and 3D transformations at near-native speeds.

The WASM component model, garbage collection support, and threading proposals are all advancing through the standardization process. Each addition makes it more feasible to port existing game engines and performance-critical code to the browser without significant compromises.

Engines like Godot and Bevy already export to WASM. Unity's web builds, while historically limited, are improving with each release. The trend is unmistakable: the tools that build native games are increasingly targeting the browser as a first-class output.

### JavaScript Engines: Relentlessly Optimized

V8 (Chrome), JavaScriptCore (Safari), and SpiderMonkey (Firefox) have been in an optimization arms race for over a decade. The result is that modern JavaScript runs orders of magnitude faster than it did when these engines launched.

For game developers using frameworks like React, Three.js, and Pixi.js, this means the "JavaScript is too slow for games" argument is essentially obsolete for the vast majority of game genres. The overhead of running in a managed runtime is measurable but rarely limiting.

## The Non-Technical Advantages

Technology parity is necessary but not sufficient. The browser's real advantages are structural — and they've always been there.

### Instant Play

Click a link. You're playing. No download. No install. No update queue. No disk space check. No account creation (unless the game chooses to require it, which we don't for first play).

This zero-friction access is impossible to replicate on any other platform. Steam requires a client. Consoles require hardware. Mobile requires an app store. The browser requires a URL.

For multiplayer games, this is transformative. Sharing a game with a friend means sending a link, not asking "do you have it installed?" Every player you want to reach is already running a browser.

### Universal Cross-Platform

A browser game runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, ChromeOS, Android, and iOS from a single codebase. Responsive design and touch input handling are solved problems in web development. You're not porting — you're building once and deploying everywhere.

In 2026, this matters more than ever. The device landscape is more fragmented than ever. Players game on laptops, tablets, phones, Chromebooks, and even smart TVs with browsers. A native game that only runs on Windows or requires a specific console is leaving most of the world's potential players behind.

### The Open Platform

The web has no gatekeeper. There's no app review process. There's no 30% platform tax. There's no exclusivity deal you need to navigate. You own your distribution, your player relationships, and your revenue.

This openness isn't just economically favorable — it's creatively liberating. We can ship experimental games, update them daily, A/B test features, and pivot quickly without asking anyone's permission.

## Netflix's Browser Pivot

It's worth noting that Netflix — one of the largest entertainment companies in the world — has been expanding its gaming efforts toward browser-based delivery. Netflix Games, which launched as mobile-only in 2021, has been exploring browser-based gaming to reach its subscribers across devices without requiring app store downloads.

This isn't a coincidence. Netflix understands distribution better than almost anyone, and they're recognizing what we've been saying: **the browser is the most efficient way to put interactive entertainment in front of the largest audience.**

When a company with Netflix's resources validates the browser as a gaming platform, it shifts the conversation for the entire industry.

## Where RMH Studios Fits

We've been building for the browser since day one — not as a compromise, but as a conviction. While other studios are discovering the browser's advantages in 2026, we've spent years understanding its constraints, optimizing for its strengths, and building a technical foundation specifically designed for web-native gaming.

Our portfolio demonstrates what's possible:

- **RMHbox** proves that real-time multiplayer party games work in the browser, with WebSocket-driven synchronization across 16+ minigames.
- **Signal Forge** proves that deep, systems-driven single-player games work in the browser, with complex state management, procedural generation, and persistent progression.
- **Neon Driftway** proves that visually ambitious 3D games work in the browser, with Three.js rendering, procedural tracks, and smooth performance.
- **Slice It!** proves that timing-critical games work in the browser, with Web Audio API precision driving rhythm gameplay.
- **RMHTube, RMH Type, and RMH Study** prove that the browser can host social, productivity, and entertainment experiences that rival native apps.

We're not experimenting with the browser. We're experts in it.

## The Remaining Gaps

Honesty demands we acknowledge what the browser still can't do as well as native platforms:

**Raw graphical fidelity.** Even with WebGPU, browser games operate within a sandbox that adds overhead compared to bare-metal native applications. For photorealistic, AAA-budget visual experiences, native still wins. For stylized, art-directed games — which is most of the indie space — the gap is negligible.

**Persistent background processing.** Browser games run in tabs, and tabs can be suspended by the browser or OS. Games that need persistent background state (like idle games ticking while you're away) require server-side logic or careful service worker implementation.

**Hardware access.** The browser's security sandbox limits access to hardware like GPUs (beyond the graphics API), local storage, and peripherals. WebXR exists for VR/AR, and the Gamepad API handles controllers, but the access is always mediated by the browser's security model.

**Discoverability.** There's no "browser game store" with the scale of Steam or the App Store. Discovery relies on organic sharing, community building, SEO, and social media. This is a solvable problem, but it's not solved yet.

## The 2026 Thesis

Here's what we believe: **2026 is the year browser gaming stops being an alternative and starts being a preference.**

Not for every game. Not for every player. But for a large and growing segment of both, the browser offers a combination of instant access, cross-platform reach, and creative freedom that no walled garden can match.

The technology is ready. The audience is there. The economics are favorable. The only question is who builds the games that prove it.

We intend to be that studio. Not because we think browser gaming is the future of *all* gaming — it's not, and pretending otherwise would be dishonest. But because we believe it's the future of *accessible* gaming, *social* gaming, and *independent* gaming.

The browser advantage in 2026 isn't theoretical. It's our shipping reality.

title: Building Virtual Worlds That Require Zero Downloads
description: How Three.js, React Three Fiber, and WebXR let us build immersive 3D worlds in a browser tab.
content:
## The Promise of Instant Worlds

There's a particular kind of magic in clicking a link and finding yourself inside a living, breathing virtual world. No installer, no progress bar, no "restart required." Just a URL, a browser tab, and a world waiting for you.

That's the experience we're building at RMH Studios, and it's made possible by a stack of web technologies that have matured dramatically over the past few years. In this post, we'll break down exactly how we build 3D virtual worlds that run entirely in the browser.

## Three.js: The Foundation

**Three.js** is the workhorse of browser-based 3D rendering. Created by Ricardo Cabello (Mr.doob) and first released in 2010, Three.js provides a high-level JavaScript API on top of WebGL (and increasingly WebGPU), abstracting away the complexity of shader programming, buffer management, and GPU communication.

For game development, Three.js gives us:

- **Scene graph management** — organizing objects, lights, cameras, and groups in a hierarchical structure
- **Material and lighting systems** — physically-based rendering (PBR), environment maps, shadow casting
- **Geometry and mesh handling** — from primitive shapes to complex imported models (glTF, FBX, OBJ)
- **Animation systems** — skeletal animation, morph targets, and keyframe interpolation
- **Post-processing** — bloom, depth of field, color grading, and custom shader effects

Three.js doesn't try to be a game engine in the way Unity or Unreal are. It's a rendering library — and that distinction is important. It gives us the building blocks without imposing an opinionated framework on top. For a studio that wants fine-grained control over every aspect of the player experience, that flexibility is invaluable.

## React Three Fiber: 3D Meets Declarative UI

Raw Three.js is powerful but imperative. You create objects, add them to scenes, manage their lifecycle manually, and handle cleanup yourself. When you're building complex interactive experiences — especially ones that need to integrate with a React-based UI layer — this imperative approach creates friction.

**React Three Fiber** (R3F) solves this elegantly. Built by the Poimandres collective, R3F is a React renderer for Three.js. It lets us describe 3D scenes declaratively using JSX, with all the benefits of React's component model: composability, state management, lifecycle hooks, and a rich ecosystem of supporting libraries.

Here's what that means in practice. Instead of manually creating a mesh, adding it to a scene, updating it on every frame, and disposing of it when it's no longer needed, we write a React component:

```jsx
function FloatingCrystal({ position }) {
  const meshRef = useRef();
  useFrame((state) => {
    meshRef.current.rotation.y = state.clock.elapsedTime * 0.5;
    meshRef.current.position.y = position[1] + Math.sin(state.clock.elapsedTime) * 0.3;
  });

  return (
    <mesh ref={meshRef} position={position}>
      <octahedronGeometry args={[0.5, 0]} />
      <meshStandardMaterial color="#8b5cf6" emissive="#4c1d95" emissiveIntensity={0.4} />
    </mesh>
  );
}
```

This component handles its own animation, cleans up automatically when unmounted, and composes naturally with other React components. The R3F ecosystem also includes libraries like **@react-three/drei** (a collection of useful abstractions) and **@react-three/postprocessing** (GPU post-processing effects), which accelerate development significantly.

Since our entire frontend stack is built on **Next.js and React**, R3F integrates seamlessly with our UI layer, routing, and state management. A 3D game scene and a 2D settings menu can live in the same component tree, sharing state naturally.

## Echoes of the Spire: A Case Study

Our game **Echoes of the Spire** is a narrative extraction thriller that takes place in atmospheric, procedurally-influenced 3D environments. Building it as a browser game meant solving several interesting technical challenges.

### Asset Loading and Streaming

A native game can assume its assets are on disk. A browser game has to download everything over the network. For Echoes of the Spire, we use aggressive asset optimization:

- **glTF with Draco compression** reduces 3D model sizes by 80-90% compared to uncompressed formats
- **Texture atlasing and compression** using basis/KTX2 formats keeps texture memory manageable
- **Progressive loading** renders the scene with low-detail placeholders while higher-quality assets stream in
- **Asset caching** via service workers ensures returning players don't re-download the same assets

### Atmospheric Rendering

The mood of Echoes of the Spire depends on atmospheric effects that are traditionally expensive: volumetric fog, dynamic lighting, particle systems. In the browser, we achieve these through a combination of:

- Custom **shader materials** written in GLSL for fog and lighting effects
- **Instanced rendering** for particle systems, keeping draw calls low even with thousands of particles
- **Baked lighting** for static environments, supplemented by a small number of dynamic lights for interactive elements

The result isn't photorealistic — and it doesn't need to be. It's atmospheric, evocative, and runs at 60fps on mid-range hardware in a browser tab.

### Performance Budgets

Browser 3D development requires disciplined performance budgeting. We set hard limits:

- **Draw calls**: under 100 per frame
- **Triangle count**: under 200K visible triangles
- **Texture memory**: under 128MB total
- **Frame time**: under 16.6ms (targeting 60fps)

Every asset, every effect, every system has to justify its cost against these budgets. This constraint actually improves our design — it forces us to find creative solutions that achieve visual impact without brute-force rendering.

## WebXR: The Future of Browser Worlds

The **WebXR Device API** is the W3C specification for accessing virtual reality and augmented reality devices through the browser. It's still early — headset adoption is growing but not yet mainstream, and the API surface is still evolving — but the implications for browser-based virtual worlds are enormous.

WebXR means that the same 3D world we render in a browser tab could, in principle, be experienced in full VR through a headset, all without a native application. The user clicks a "Enter VR" button, puts on their headset, and they're *inside* the world they were just viewing on their screen.

We're not shipping VR experiences today, but our architecture is WebXR-aware. Our camera systems, interaction models, and scene structures are designed with the possibility of VR in mind. When the headset install base reaches critical mass, we want to be ready.

## Why This Matters

Building 3D virtual worlds in the browser isn't just a technical curiosity — it's a philosophical statement about accessibility. Every virtual world that requires a download, an install, a specific operating system, or a particular piece of hardware is a world with a velvet rope.

**Browser-based virtual worlds have no velvet rope.** If you have a browser, you're in. That democratization of access is what drives us at RMH Studios. We're building worlds that anyone can visit, instantly, from anywhere.

The technology to make those worlds beautiful, immersive, and performant exists today. Three.js, React Three Fiber, WebGPU, and WebXR are the tools. The only thing left is the craft to wield them well. That's what we show up to do every day.

title: Building a Discord Community From Scratch
description: How RMH Studios grew a Discord community using bots, daily games, and a commitment to genuine connection.
content:
## Why Discord Is Our Living Room

Every game studio needs a home — not just a website, but a **living space** where players, developers, and fans coexist in real time. For RMH Studios, that home is Discord.

Discord reported over **200 million monthly active users** by mid-2024, up from 150 million in 2022. It's no longer just a voice chat app for gamers. It's the default community platform for indie studios, content creators, open-source projects, and increasingly, mainstream brands. For an indie game studio building browser-based games, Discord is where our players already are.

But having a Discord server and having a **community** are different things. A server is infrastructure. A community is people who choose to spend their time with you and with each other. Building the latter requires intention, consistency, and a willingness to be present.

This is what we've learned.

## Starting From Zero

Our Discord server launched with exactly two members: us. No audience, no mailing list, no viral tweet driving signups. The early days were quiet — and that quietness was valuable.

We used the empty period to establish **tone and structure** before anyone arrived. We set up channels, wrote welcome messages, defined rules, and — critically — decided what kind of community we wanted to be. Not every server needs to be the same. Some are chaotic and memey. Some are professional and focused. Ours aims for something in between: **welcoming, curious, and game-obsessed**.

The first members came from personal networks and early playtesters. These founding members shaped the culture more than any moderation policy could. They set the standard for how people talk to each other, what kinds of conversations happen, and how newcomers are received.

### The Cold Start Problem

The hardest phase of community building is the **cold start** — when there aren't enough members to sustain organic conversation. A new member joins, sees an empty chat, and leaves. Without a critical mass of active participants, the server feels dead.

We solved the cold start problem with three strategies:

1. **Developer presence.** In the early months, we (the developers) were the most active members. We shared development updates, asked for opinions, posted behind-the-scenes content, and responded to every message. Players who joined found active conversation because we made sure there was always something happening.

2. **Structured daily content.** We created recurring events — daily game challenges, weekly feedback threads, development livestreams — that gave members a reason to check in regularly. Even with few members, a daily challenge creates a rhythm that makes the server feel alive.

3. **Bot-driven engagement.** This is where our custom Discord bots became essential.

## RMHdle and RMHConnections: Games as Community Tools

Our most effective community engagement tools aren't announcements or update posts — they're **games**.

**RMHdle** is our daily word puzzle, inspired by the Wordle phenomenon that captivated the internet in 2022. Each day, a new puzzle is posted automatically. Members submit their guesses and share their results (using the now-familiar colored square grid) without spoiling the answer. The daily puzzle creates a shared ritual — something members do every morning, a reason to open Discord.

**RMHConnections** is our take on the NYT Connections format — a daily grouping puzzle where players categorize 16 items into four hidden groups. Like RMHdle, it's posted automatically each day and generates conversation as members discuss strategies and compare results.

These aren't just games — they're **conversation starters**. The daily puzzles generate more discussion than any announcement we've ever posted. Members debate strategies, celebrate streaks, commiserate over tough puzzles, and develop friendly rivalries over who solves them fastest. The puzzles transform passive members into active participants.

### Building the Bots

Both RMHdle and RMHConnections run as custom Discord bots built with the Discord.js library. The bots handle:

- **Puzzle generation** — creating or selecting daily puzzles from a curated database
- **Submission tracking** — recording guesses and maintaining streaks
- **Leaderboards** — weekly and monthly rankings that drive competition
- **Spoiler prevention** — ensuring answers aren't visible until the puzzle period ends
- **Statistics** — tracking community-wide solve rates and difficulty ratings

The bots were surprisingly affordable to build and run. Discord's bot API is well-documented, hosting costs are minimal for a bot serving hundreds of users, and the engagement return is enormous relative to the investment.

## Channel Architecture

How you organize a Discord server matters more than most people think. Channel structure determines what conversations happen, where they happen, and whether new members can find their way around.

Our server uses a deliberate channel architecture:

**Welcome zone:**
- `#welcome` — automated greeting with server rules and role selection
- `#introductions` — new members introduce themselves (prompted by the welcome message)

**Game channels:**
- One channel per game (e.g., `#rmhbox`, `#signal-forge`, `#slice-it`)
- `#daily-games` — RMHdle and RMHConnections live here
- `#game-feedback` — structured feedback for any game

**Community channels:**
- `#general` — open conversation
- `#off-topic` — non-gaming discussion
- `#share-your-stuff` — members share their own creative work

**Development channels:**
- `#dev-updates` — official development news (announcements-style, limited posting)
- `#dev-log` — informal development progress, screenshots, questions
- `#bug-reports` — structured bug reporting with templates

The key principle is **channel separation by intent**. A player looking for game tips shouldn't have to scroll through bug reports. A developer posting an update shouldn't compete with general chat. Each channel has a clear purpose, and we gently redirect off-topic messages to the right place.

## Moderation Lessons

We've learned several moderation lessons the hard way:

**Rules must be specific.** "Be respectful" is a good principle but a bad rule. It's unenforceable because everyone's definition of "respectful" differs. Our rules specify concrete behaviors: no personal attacks, no spam, no NSFW content, no unsolicited promotion. Specific rules create clear expectations.

**Moderation must be consistent.** Inconsistent enforcement breeds resentment. If one member is warned for something another member did without consequence, trust erodes. We maintain a moderation log and review it regularly to ensure consistency.

**Automoderation handles volume; humans handle nuance.** We use Discord's AutoMod for keyword filtering, spam detection, and link restrictions. But context-dependent moderation — is this joke offensive or playful? Is this criticism constructive or hostile? — requires human judgment. Our moderators are empowered to use judgment, and we back their decisions.

**Escalation paths matter.** Members need a way to report issues privately. Our server has a `#mod-mail` channel where members can message the moderation team confidentially. This prevents public confrontations and gives moderators time to investigate before acting.

## What Doesn't Work

Transparency means sharing failures, not just successes. Here's what didn't work for us:

**Excessive channel creation.** We initially created channels for every possible topic. Most sat empty, making the server feel abandoned. We consolidated aggressively, and activity concentrated in fewer, busier channels. Empty channels are worse than missing channels.

**Forced engagement.** Early on, we tried assigning "daily discussion questions" to spark conversation. It felt artificial, and members didn't engage. Organic conversation sparked by game updates and daily puzzles was far more effective than manufactured prompts.

**Giveaways for growth.** We experimented with "invite friends for a chance to win" promotions. They drove signups but not retention. Members who join for a giveaway leave after it ends. Organic growth through genuine interest produces more valuable community members than incentivized growth.

## Growing Beyond the Server

As our community grows, we're thinking about how Discord fits into a broader community ecosystem. Discord is excellent for real-time conversation but poor for long-form content, documentation, or searchable knowledge. We're exploring how to extend community interaction through:

- **Our blog** (you're reading it) for long-form development content
- **In-game community features** like shared leaderboards and player profiles
- **Community events** like game jams and tournaments that originate in Discord but play out in our games

The Discord server remains the heartbeat — the place where members connect daily, share ideas, and feel like part of something. But a healthy community eventually outgrows any single platform.

## The Real Community Metric

We don't measure community health by member count. We measure it by **daily active participants** — the number of unique members who post, react, or engage each day. A server with 10,000 members and 20 daily participants is a ghost town. A server with 500 members and 100 daily participants is thriving.

Our daily puzzles, consistent developer presence, and genuine engagement have built something we're proud of: a community where people know each other's names, celebrate each other's achievements, and show up every day not because they have to, but because they want to.

That's what community means. And we're building it one daily puzzle at a time.

title: Can Browser Games Compete with Steam?
description: An honest assessment of browser distribution vs. the world's dominant PC gaming platform.
content:
## The Giant in the Room

In January 2025, Steam hit a new record: **34.6 million peak concurrent users.** Valve's platform hosts over 70,000 games, processes billions of dollars in transactions annually, and has become so dominant that "launching on Steam" is synonymous with "launching on PC" for most developers.

So when we say we're building games for the browser instead of Steam, people look at us like we've lost our minds.

We haven't. But we also aren't going to pretend the answer to "Can browser games compete with Steam?" is a simple yes. It's more nuanced than that, and we believe in giving you the honest version.

## What Steam Does Well

Credit where it's due. Steam is dominant for real reasons:

**Discovery and storefronts.** Steam's recommendation algorithm, curated lists, and seasonal sales create a discovery engine that no browser game platform can match today. Getting featured on the Steam front page can generate millions of dollars in revenue overnight.

**Community infrastructure.** Steam reviews, forums, Workshop mod support, and the Steam Community provide a built-in social layer that developers get for free. This infrastructure took Valve decades and enormous resources to build.

**Trust and payments.** Hundreds of millions of people have their credit cards saved in Steam. The purchase friction is near zero for returning customers. Refund policies provide consumer protection. Players trust Steam with their money.

**Achievements, cloud saves, and profiles.** The meta-game of Steam — your profile, your game library, your achievement showcase — creates switching costs that keep players on the platform.

We respect all of this. We'd be foolish not to.

## Where Steam Falls Short

But Steam's dominance comes with real costs, and they're costs that indie developers feel acutely.

### The 30% Cut

Steam takes 30% of every sale for games earning under $10 million. That's not a fee — it's nearly a third of your revenue. For an indie studio operating on thin margins, that 30% can be the difference between sustainability and shutdown.

Epic Games Store launched in 2018 explicitly to challenge this, offering an 88/12 split. Despite spending hundreds of millions on exclusives and free game giveaways, Epic has struggled to dent Steam's market share meaningfully — a testament to how entrenched Steam's network effects are. But the debate Epic started was important: **the 30% platform tax is not an immutable law of nature.**

Browser games pay 0% to a platform holder. Our revenue share with payment processors (Stripe charges ~2.9% + 30¢ per transaction) is a fraction of what Steam takes.

### The Discovery Problem

Steam's discovery algorithm is a double-edged sword. Yes, it can surface your game to millions. But it can also bury it. With thousands of games launching every month, the median Steam game earns very little. A 2023 analysis by game industry consultant Simon Carless estimated that the median indie game on Steam earns around $1,500 in its first month.

The platform giveth, and the platform taketh away. You're building on someone else's land.

### The Download Barrier

Every Steam game requires downloading a client, then downloading the game itself. For a AAA title, this is expected. For a casual party game or a quick puzzle experience, it's a significant barrier. No one downloads a Steam client to play a five-minute typing race.

Browser games load in seconds. The entire concept of "installation" doesn't exist. You click a link, you're playing. This zero-friction access is our single greatest competitive advantage.

## The Itch.io Alternative

It's worth mentioning itch.io, which has carved out a genuine niche as the indie-friendliest game platform. Itch.io lets developers set their own revenue split (including 0%), supports browser-based games directly, and has fostered an incredible community of experimental and jam games.

But itch.io's audience is relatively small and skews heavily toward other developers and game jam enthusiasts. It's an amazing community platform, but it's not a commercial distribution channel for most studios.

We admire what itch.io represents — open, developer-first, low-friction — and we're essentially building on those same principles, but with our own domain, our own community, and our own infrastructure.

## The Browser's Unique Advantages

Here's where we get specific about why browser distribution isn't just "Steam but worse":

### Zero Friction, Truly

We keep saying this because it's the foundation of everything. When someone shares an RMHbox link in a group chat, every recipient is one click from playing. Not "one click from a store page where they might create an account, enter payment info, download a client, download the game, and then play." One click from **playing**.

This matters enormously for multiplayer games. The host doesn't need to check whether everyone has the game installed. They send a link. Everyone joins. The game starts.

### Cross-Platform by Default

A browser game runs on Windows, Mac, Linux, ChromeOS, and mobile devices (with responsive design) from a single codebase. Steam supports Windows, Mac, and Linux, but each requires separate builds and testing. Console ports require entirely separate development efforts.

We write our games once. They run everywhere a modern browser runs.

### Instant Updates

When we fix a bug or ship a feature, every player gets it on their next page load. No download queues, no update prompts, no "restarting to apply update." The deploy-to-player pipeline is measured in minutes, not days.

### Linkability

Every game, every room, every session can have a unique URL. This makes sharing, embedding, streaming, and integrating with other platforms trivially easy. You can't deep-link into a Steam game from a Discord message and have it launch instantly.

## The Honest Tradeoffs

We promised an honest assessment, so here's what we give up by choosing the browser:

**Performance ceiling.** Browser games can't match the raw performance of native applications. WebGL and WebGPU are impressive, but they're running inside a browser sandbox. For our games — party games, card games, puzzle games, 2D and stylized 3D — this doesn't matter. For a photorealistic open-world RPG, it would.

**No built-in storefront discovery.** We don't have Steam's algorithm sending players our way. Our growth depends on organic sharing, community building, and eventually our own marketing. This is harder, but it means our relationship with players is direct, not mediated by a platform.

**Offline play is limited.** Browser games generally require an internet connection. Service workers and progressive web app techniques can enable some offline functionality, but it's not the same as a fully offline native game.

**Perception.** There's still a stigma that browser games are "lesser" — that real games are downloaded executables. This perception is changing, but it hasn't fully changed yet. Every game we ship that surprises people with its quality helps erode that stigma.

## Not Competing — Expanding

Here's our real thesis: **we're not trying to compete with Steam. We're trying to reach the players Steam can't.**

Steam serves an audience that's willing to install a client, browse a store, make purchases, and manage a library. That's a huge audience, but it's not everyone.

We're serving the audience that clicks a link in a group chat. The audience that wants to play something *right now* without committing to anything. The audience that's on a Chromebook at school, or a work laptop without admin privileges, or a phone without storage space.

These audiences have been underserved by the gaming industry for years. Flash games served them once, and when Flash died in December 2020, a gap opened. io games like Agar.io and Slither.io partially filled it, proving that browser games could attract millions of concurrent players.

We're building the next generation to fill that gap — with richer experiences, better technology, and a studio behind them that's in it for the long run.

## The Bottom Line

Can browser games compete with Steam? Not on Steam's terms. But on our terms — instant access, zero friction, cross-platform, community-driven, developer-owned — the browser offers something Steam fundamentally cannot.

We're not trying to be the next Steam. We're trying to make Steam optional.

title: Dark Mode: A Deep Dive into CSS Variable Architecture
description: A detailed look at the UI redesign process for Dark Mode: A Deep Dive into CSS Variable Architecture.
content:
UI design is never finished. It evolves. For Dark Mode: A Deep Dive into CSS Variable Architecture, our goal was to bridge the gap between functionality and aesthetic brilliance. In this post, we'll dive deep into the specific challenges we faced and the solutions we implemented.

## The Objective

The primary goal of the Dark Mode: A Deep Dive into CSS Variable Architecture redesign was to enhance user engagement while maintaining technical efficiency. We wanted users to feel a sense of fluid interaction, where every click or hover provided meaningful feedback. This required a fundamental rethink of our existing components and spiritual alignment with the overall rmhstudios brand.

We started by auditing our current implementation. What was working? What felt clunky? What was redundant? By stripping back the unnecessary elements, we were able to focus on the core user journey. For Dark Mode: A Deep Dive into CSS Variable Architecture, this meant focusing on clarity and speed.

## Iterative Design Process

Design at rmhstudios is iterative. We don't just build once and ship. We prototype, test, and refine. For this particular update, we went through three major stages of development:

1.  **Conceptual Wireframing**: Mapping out the raw data flow and user interaction points. We ignored visuals at this stage to focus purely on UX.
2.  **Visual Prototyping**: Applying our signature neon aesthetic. This involved heavy use of CSS variables to ensure consistency across light and dark modes.
3.  **Technical Integration**: Merging the design with our Next.js and Tailwind architecture. Performance was a key metric here—we couldn't let visual polish compromise load times.

One specific challenge was handling responsive layout shifts. As screens get smaller, information density needs to be managed carefully. We implemented custom media queries and flexible grid systems to ensure that Dark Mode: A Deep Dive into CSS Variable Architecture looks stunning on everything from a widescreen monitor to a handheld device.

## Key Technical Details

From a technical perspective, we relied heavily on modern CSS features. We used `clamp()` for fluid typography and `grid-template-areas` for complex layouts. This allowed us to write cleaner code that is easier to maintain. We also leaned into the power of Framer Motion for micro-animations. These subtle movements—like a button gently pulsing or a card sliding into view—add a layer of polish that makes the web feel alive.

We also prioritized accessibility. Every redesign is an opportunity to improve. We audited our color contrast ratios and ensured that all interactive elements are keyboard-navigable. A beautiful UI is useless if it's not inclusive.

## The Impact

Since implementing these changes, we've seen a noticeable improvement in user retention and satisfaction. The feedback has been overwhelmingly positive. Users appreciate the cleaner aesthetic and the more intuitive navigation. It's a testament to the idea that small, thoughtful changes can have a massive impact on the overall experience.

We are particularly proud of the performance gains. By optimizing our asset delivery and refining our component logic, we've managed to reduce the cumulative layout shift (CLS) and improve the largest contentful paint (LCP) across the site.

## Looking Forward

This is just the beginning. As we move closer to the launch of Project Vega and further updates to our existing games, we'll continue to push the boundaries of what's possible in web-based game UI. We're already experimenting with more advanced shader effects and interactive 3D elements that sit directly in the DOM.

The journey of design is continuous. We're always learning, always growing, and always looking for ways to make rmhstudios.com the best it can be. Stay tuned for more updates as we continue to refine our vision.

## Conclusion

The redesign of Dark Mode: A Deep Dive into CSS Variable Architecture represents a significant milestone in our design evolution. It's not just about making things look "pretty"; it's about creating an experience that is functional, accessible, and delightful. We hope this deep dive into our process provides some insight into how we approach UI at rmhstudios.

What are your thoughts on this new direction? Do you have suggestions for future redesigns? We'd love to hear from you in the community Discord or via our feedback forms. Your input helps shape the future of this studio.

Thanks for reading, and keep on creating!

title: Strike, Block, Prepare, Probe: The Four-Action System
description: How we designed Cursed Logic around just four actions and created surprising strategic depth from elegant simplicity.
content:
## The Tyranny of Choice

Most games give you more options as they progress. More abilities, more items, more buttons to press, more menus to navigate. There's a comfort in that — a feeling of growth and power. But there's also a cost. When you have twenty options, the weight of any single choice is diluted. You can always fall back on something else.

Cursed Logic goes in the opposite direction. **You have four actions. That's it. Forever.** Strike, Block, Prepare, Probe. No unlockable fifth action. No secret techniques. Four choices, every round, for the rest of your time with the game.

This was the scariest design decision I made, and also the best one.

## The Four Pillars

Let me break down each action and what it represents in the tactical ecosystem:

### Strike

Strike is damage. It's the sword swing, the bullet, the punch. When you Strike, you're committing to aggression. You're saying: "I believe you are not blocking, and I'm willing to bet my round on that."

Strike is powerful against Prepare (catching the opponent in a vulnerable state) and against Probe (punishing information-gathering with direct damage). But Strike bounces harmlessly off a Block. A well-timed Block turns your aggression into wasted initiative.

### Block

Block is defense with teeth. When you Block successfully against a Strike, you negate the damage and gain a small positional advantage — the feeling of having "read" your opponent correctly. But Block against Prepare does nothing useful. You've played safe while your opponent powers up.

Block is the action that teaches patience. New players either Block too much (and get overwhelmed by a Preparing opponent) or too little (and get shredded by Strikes). Finding the right Block frequency is one of the first skill curves in Cursed Logic.

### Prepare

Prepare is investment. When you Prepare, you're building up your stance — charging energy that makes your future actions more powerful. A Strike after a Prepare hits harder. A Block after a Prepare absorbs more effectively. Prepare is the action that says: "I'm willing to sacrifice this round to win the next one."

The risk is enormous. Preparing leaves you vulnerable to Strikes. If the Protocol reads your Prepare and Strikes, you take damage *and* lose your initiative. Prepare is a gamble on the future, and it's where the deepest strategic decisions live.

### Probe

Probe is unique because **only the player has it.** The Protocol cannot Probe. This asymmetry is fundamental to the game's design. Probe lets you peek at the Protocol's projected state — its likely next actions, its current mode, its confidence level. It's information warfare.

But Probe has a cost: you're spending a round not dealing damage, not defending, and not building stance. Every Probe is a round where the Protocol acts and you just... watch. The information you gain has to be *worth* more than the action you gave up.

## The Interaction Matrix

Here's where the four actions become a rich strategic system. The interactions between them create a web of decisions that's deeper than it appears:

```
              Strike    Block     Prepare   Probe
Strike        Clash     Blocked   Hits!     Hits!
Block         Blocks!   Nothing   Nothing   Nothing
Prepare       Hit!      Builds    Both Prep N/A
Probe         Hit!      Info      Info      N/A
```

This isn't a simple rock-paper-scissors triangle. It's a **four-node interaction graph** with asymmetric outcomes:

- Strike beats Prepare and Probe but loses to Block
- Block beats Strike but wastes against Prepare and Probe
- Prepare loses to Strike but builds power against everything else
- Probe sacrifices action for information regardless of opponent's choice

The asymmetry of Probe — available only to the player — is what elevates this beyond a matching game. The Protocol has to play without information. You have the *option* of information, but at a cost. This creates a fundamental strategic tension: **do you play the game, or do you study the opponent?**

## Depth Through Simplicity

I studied a lot of competitive games while designing this system. Here's what I learned:

**The best fighting games aren't decided by combo execution — they're decided by decisions.** A Street Fighter match at the highest level is about reading your opponent's tendencies and making split-second decisions based on spacing, meter, and psychological pressure. The buttons are just how you express those decisions.

Cursed Logic strips away the execution entirely. There are no combos. No frame data. No spacing. Just the decision. This means that **every player, regardless of mechanical skill, is competing on the same axis: prediction and adaptation.**

### Information Asymmetry Creates Drama

One of the most important design principles in Cursed Logic is that the player always has *slightly* more information than the Protocol. You can Probe. You can see the Protocol's health. You know its current variant. You have tools the Protocol doesn't.

But — and this is critical — the Protocol has something you don't: **consistency.** It doesn't get nervous. It doesn't tilt after three bad rounds. It doesn't make emotional decisions. The Protocol plays its strategy cleanly, every time. Your information advantage is balanced against its emotional stability.

This creates a dynamic where skilled players win by leveraging their information tools effectively, while the Protocol wins against players who let frustration or overconfidence cloud their decision-making. Cursed Logic tests your ability to stay rational under pressure as much as it tests your ability to predict.

## The Lesson of Constraints

Working with only four actions taught me something profound about game design: **constraints breed creativity.** When I couldn't add a fifth action to solve a balance problem, I had to dig deeper into the interactions between the existing four. When a particular matchup felt stale, the solution wasn't "add more stuff" — it was "examine why the existing stuff isn't creating interesting choices."

The mutation system (which modifies how actions work each round) emerged directly from this constraint. Instead of adding new actions, we change how the *existing* actions behave. Strike might deal double damage one round. Block might reflect damage. Prepare might grant extra stance charge. The four actions become a canvas, and mutations paint new pictures on that canvas every duel.

### Playtest Revelations

During early playtesting, I was terrified that four actions wouldn't be enough. Players would get bored, I thought. They'd solve the game in an hour and move on. What actually happened was the opposite.

Players developed **personal styles.** Some became aggressive Strikers who relied on reading Block timing. Others became patient Preparers who built up devastating powered attacks. Some became Probe addicts, gathering information obsessively before committing to action. And a few became unpredictable wildcards who randomized their own play to prevent the Protocol from reading them.

Four actions. Infinite strategies. That's the magic of Cursed Logic's core system.

## Moving Forward

The four-action system has proven more robust than I ever hoped. It supports the stance system, the mutation system, the Protocol variants, and the shop upgrades — all without needing to grow. Every new feature we add is a layer *on top of* the four actions, not a replacement for them.

If Cursed Logic has taught me one thing as a designer, it's this: find your core. Make it small. Make it elegant. And then build everything else around it.

*Four choices. Infinite outcomes. Duel the Protocol.*

title: Cyber-Noir Aesthetics: Designing the Cursed Logic UI
description: How deep black backgrounds, cyan and amber accents, and monospace fonts create Cursed Logic's mysterious hacker atmosphere.
content:
## Atmosphere Is Gameplay

There's a school of thought that says visuals are separate from gameplay. That a game with programmer art can be just as compelling as one with beautiful design. I understand this argument, and there's truth in it — a great core loop is a great core loop regardless of how it looks.

But Cursed Logic taught me that this separation is false for certain types of games. **In a game about psychological tension, atmosphere IS gameplay.** The way the interface looks, moves, and feels directly influences how players think and decide. The UI isn't a skin over the game — it's part of the duel.

When you sit down to face the Protocol, you should feel like you're jacking into a hostile system. The screen should feel like a terminal. The text should feel like code.
