title: Particle Effects as UI: Enhancing Feedback Visuals
description: A detailed look at the UI redesign process for Particle Effects as UI: Enhancing Feedback Visuals.
content:
UI design is never finished. It evolves. For Particle Effects as UI: Enhancing Feedback Visuals, our goal was to bridge the gap between functionality and aesthetic brilliance. In this post, we'll dive deep into the specific challenges we faced and the solutions we implemented.

## The Objective

The primary goal of the Particle Effects as UI: Enhancing Feedback Visuals redesign was to enhance user engagement while maintaining technical efficiency. We wanted users to feel a sense of fluid interaction, where every click or hover provided meaningful feedback. This required a fundamental rethink of our existing components and spiritual alignment with the overall rmhstudios brand.

We started by auditing our current implementation. What was working? What felt clunky? What was redundant? By stripping back the unnecessary elements, we were able to focus on the core user journey. For Particle Effects as UI: Enhancing Feedback Visuals, this meant focusing on clarity and speed.

## Iterative Design Process

Design at rmhstudios is iterative. We don't just build once and ship. We prototype, test, and refine. For this particular update, we went through three major stages of development:

1.  **Conceptual Wireframing**: Mapping out the raw data flow and user interaction points. We ignored visuals at this stage to focus purely on UX.
2.  **Visual Prototyping**: Applying our signature neon aesthetic. This involved heavy use of CSS variables to ensure consistency across light and dark modes.
3.  **Technical Integration**: Merging the design with our Next.js and Tailwind architecture. Performance was a key metric here—we couldn't let visual polish compromise load times.

One specific challenge was handling responsive layout shifts. As screens get smaller, information density needs to be managed carefully. We implemented custom media queries and flexible grid systems to ensure that Particle Effects as UI: Enhancing Feedback Visuals looks stunning on everything from a widescreen monitor to a handheld device.

## Key Technical Details

From a technical perspective, we relied heavily on modern CSS features. We used `clamp()` for fluid typography and `grid-template-areas` for complex layouts. This allowed us to write cleaner code that is easier to maintain. We also leaned into the power of Framer Motion for micro-animations. These subtle movements—like a button gently pulsing or a card sliding into view—add a layer of polish that makes the web feel alive.

We also prioritized accessibility. Every redesign is an opportunity to improve. We audited our color contrast ratios and ensured that all interactive elements are keyboard-navigable. A beautiful UI is useless if it's not inclusive.

## The Impact

Since implementing these changes, we've seen a noticeable improvement in user retention and satisfaction. The feedback has been overwhelmingly positive. Users appreciate the cleaner aesthetic and the more intuitive navigation. It's a testament to the idea that small, thoughtful changes can have a massive impact on the overall experience.

We are particularly proud of the performance gains. By optimizing our asset delivery and refining our component logic, we've managed to reduce the cumulative layout shift (CLS) and improve the largest contentful paint (LCP) across the site.

## Looking Forward

This is just the beginning. As we move closer to the launch of Project Vega and further updates to our existing games, we'll continue to push the boundaries of what's possible in web-based game UI. We're already experimenting with more advanced shader effects and interactive 3D elements that sit directly in the DOM.

The journey of design is continuous. We're always learning, always growing, and always looking for ways to make rmhstudios.com the best it can be. Stay tuned for more updates as we continue to refine our vision.

## Conclusion

The redesign of Particle Effects as UI: Enhancing Feedback Visuals represents a significant milestone in our design evolution. It's not just about making things look "pretty"; it's about creating an experience that is functional, accessible, and delightful. We hope this deep dive into our process provides some insight into how we approach UI at rmhstudios.

What are your thoughts on this new direction? Do you have suggestions for future redesigns? We'd love to hear from you in the community Discord or via our feedback forms. Your input helps shape the future of this studio.

Thanks for reading, and keep on creating!

title: How Player Feedback Shapes Every RMH Game
description: Why we treat player feedback as a development tool, not a suggestion box — and how it changes our games.
content:
## The Players Know Things We Don't

There's a moment in every game's development when the team realizes something humbling: **the players understand the game differently than the developers do**. We know how we intended the game to work. They know how it actually feels. Those two things are often surprisingly different.

At RMH Studios, player feedback isn't a box we check before launch. It's a **continuous development input** — an ongoing conversation that shapes features, adjusts difficulty, and occasionally redirects entire design decisions. We've built systems and habits around feedback collection because we've learned, repeatedly, that the gap between developer intent and player experience is where the most important design work happens.

## The Valve Standard

When we think about playtesting culture, we think about **Valve**. The company's approach to playtesting, documented in numerous GDC talks and developer interviews over the years, set a standard that the industry still aspires to. Valve's playtesting philosophy can be summarized in one principle: **if the player is confused, the game is wrong** — not the player.

This sounds obvious, but it's surprisingly difficult to internalize. When a developer watches a playtester miss an "obvious" clue or fail at an "easy" challenge, the natural instinct is to think the player isn't trying hard enough. Valve's discipline was to resist that instinct and instead ask: what about the game led to this failure?

The development of *Portal* is a famous example. Early playtests revealed that players didn't understand what portals were, how they worked, or what to do with them. The development team's response wasn't to add a tutorial text dump — it was to redesign the opening sequence so that the mechanics taught themselves through guided play. Every "aha" moment in Portal's first hour was designed in response to playtest confusion.

We don't have Valve's resources, but we've adopted their mindset: **player confusion is a design signal, not a player failing**.

## Our Feedback Architecture

We collect player feedback through three primary channels, each serving a different purpose:

### The Public Feedback Board

Our feedback board is a structured system where players submit feature requests, bug reports, and general suggestions. Each submission is categorized (bug, feature, balance, UX) and tagged with the relevant game. Players can upvote existing submissions, creating a natural prioritization system.

The feedback board solves a specific problem: **aggregation**. In Discord, a great suggestion might get posted, discussed briefly, and then scroll away. On the feedback board, it persists. Ten players independently requesting the same feature results in a single item with ten upvotes — a clear signal of demand.

We review the feedback board weekly. High-upvote items get discussed in our planning sessions. We update items with our response — whether we're implementing the suggestion, considering it, or declining it (with explanation). Closing the loop is essential; a feedback board where nothing ever gets addressed is worse than no board at all.

### Discord Conversations

The Discord server provides what the feedback board cannot: **nuance and context**. When a player says "the controls feel weird" on a feedback form, it's useful but imprecise. When the same player describes the issue in Discord and three others chime in with "yes, exactly, especially when..." — that conversation reveals the actual problem.

We participate actively in these conversations. Not to defend design decisions, but to understand them. Questions like "what were you trying to do when it felt wrong?" and "how would you expect it to work?" generate insights that structured feedback forms rarely capture.

### Gameplay Analytics

The third feedback channel is silent: **telemetry data**. With player consent, our games collect anonymized gameplay data that reveals patterns no player would think to report:

- **Drop-off points** — where do players stop playing? A sudden spike in session endings at a specific game moment indicates a frustration barrier.
- **Feature usage** — which features do players actually use? We've shipped features we were proud of that telemetry revealed nobody touched.
- **Difficulty curves** — what's the actual win rate at each difficulty level? Designer intuition about difficulty is frequently wrong.
- **Session duration** — how long do players play? Unexpectedly short sessions indicate the game isn't holding attention. Unexpectedly long sessions might indicate addictive patterns we should examine.

Telemetry doesn't replace direct feedback — it complements it. A player might report "the game is too hard" without being able to articulate where. Telemetry shows us exactly where the difficulty spike is. Combined, these signals are much more actionable than either alone.

## Real Examples of Feedback-Driven Changes

Abstract principles are less useful than concrete examples. Here are real changes we made based on player feedback:

### Signal Forge: The Deck Size Debate

Signal Forge launched with a maximum deck size of 30 cards. Our design rationale was that smaller decks created more strategic decisions — every card inclusion meant a meaningful exclusion.

Players disagreed. Feedback consistently reported that 30 cards felt restrictive, especially after unlocking rare cards that they wanted to include but couldn't without removing favorites. The feedback wasn't "make the game easier" — it was "let me express my strategy more fully."

We increased the maximum deck size to 40. Win rates didn't change significantly (the game wasn't made easier), but player satisfaction scores increased markedly. Players felt more ownership over their decks. The 30-card limit was a design preference that conflicted with player expression.

### RMHbox: The Timer Controversy

Several RMHbox minigames originally used a visible countdown timer. The timer created urgency, which we thought was exciting. Players reported something different: **anxiety**. Specifically, players with gaming anxiety (a more common condition than many developers realize) found the ticking timer stressful rather than exciting.

We changed the timer to a **progress indicator** — a bar that fills rather than a number that counts down. The mechanical effect is identical (you still have the same amount of time), but the psychological effect is different. A filling bar feels like progress. A counting timer feels like pressure. Player satisfaction for time-limited minigames increased by 23% after this change.

### Slice It!: The Calibration Discovery

Early Slice It! feedback included a persistent complaint: "the timing feels off." We knew audio-visual sync was critical and had invested heavily in the rhythm engine. Were players wrong?

No. We were wrong — but not about the engine. The issue was that we hadn't included a **per-device calibration system**. Our engine was precisely synced to the audio clock, but the audio clock's relationship to the speaker output varied by device. A player with Bluetooth headphones experienced 100ms+ of audio latency that our engine didn't account for.

We built the calibration system described in our Slice It! engineering post entirely because players told us the timing felt wrong. They couldn't diagnose the technical cause, but their experience was accurate. The game was off — on their device, with their audio setup. Trusting their experience over our technical confidence led to a fundamental improvement.

### Neon Driftway: The Color Accessibility Issue

A player with deuteranopia (red-green color blindness) reported that certain obstacle types in Neon Driftway were indistinguishable from the background. Our neon color palette, which looked vibrant to us, created dangerous invisibility for colorblind players.

We added a **high-contrast mode** that replaces color-dependent distinctions with shape-dependent ones. Obstacles that were previously differentiated by color (red vs. green) are now also differentiated by shape (circle vs. triangle). This change benefited not just colorblind players but anyone playing in bright environments where screen colors wash out.

## The Feedback Mindset

Collecting feedback is a system. **Acting on feedback** is a mindset. Here are the principles that guide how we process feedback:

**Volume matters more than individual reports.** A single player asking for a feature is an opinion. Twenty players asking for the same feature is a signal. We resist the urge to react to every individual piece of feedback and instead look for patterns.

**Understand the problem, not the solution.** Players are experts at identifying what feels wrong. They're not always experts at identifying the fix. When a player suggests "add a minimap," the real feedback might be "I feel lost." The solution might be a minimap, or better landmarks, or a compass, or improved level layout. We dig for the underlying problem.

**Explain decisions.** When we decline feedback, we explain why. "We considered this but chose not to implement it because..." is respectful and builds trust. Players who understand the reasoning behind decisions are more likely to offer constructive feedback in the future, even when their suggestions aren't adopted.

**Close the loop.** When we implement a change based on feedback, we credit the community. "This change was inspired by player feedback" appears in our patch notes regularly. People who see their input acknowledged contribute more.

## The Cost of Not Listening

We've also experienced what happens when feedback is ignored too long. Early versions of Temple of Joy had a prestige mechanic that players consistently reported was confusing. We believed the confusion was temporary — that players would understand the system once they spent more time with it. Three months of persistent feedback proved us wrong. The mechanic was genuinely unclear, and our reluctance to change it cost us player retention during a critical growth period.

The lesson: **feedback has an expiration date**. Address it while the players are still engaged enough to care. Wait too long, and they leave — taking their insights with them.

## Feedback as Development Practice

At RMH Studios, feedback review is a scheduled development activity, not something we do when we have time. Every sprint includes a feedback review session where we:

1. Review new feedback board submissions and Discord threads
2. Analyze telemetry data for emerging patterns
3. Prioritize feedback-driven changes against our roadmap
4. Close resolved feedback items with explanations

This discipline ensures that player voices are heard consistently, not just during launch weeks or crises. Games are conversations between developers and players. The developers who listen best build the games that last.

We build games for players. It would be strange not to ask them what they think.

title: Why We Built a Social Study App
description: How RMH Study turns the Pomodoro Technique into a shared, accountable experience — and why body-doubling actually works.
content:
## A Tomato-Shaped Timer Changed Everything

In the late 1980s, a university student named Francesco Cirillo grabbed a tomato-shaped kitchen timer and changed the way millions of people think about focus. His **Pomodoro Technique** — work for 25 minutes, break for 5, repeat — became one of the most widely adopted productivity systems in the world. Cirillo published his foundational paper in 1992 and later expanded it into a full methodology, but the core idea has always been disarmingly simple: time-box your attention, and your attention improves.

We've used the Pomodoro Technique ourselves for years. As indie developers juggling game design, networking code, audio engineering, and marketing, we live and die by our ability to focus. But we noticed something: the technique works better when you're not alone.

## "Study With Me" and the Rise of Co-Working Streams

If you've spent any time on YouTube in the past five years, you've seen them — "study with me" livestreams where someone sits at a desk, studies silently for hours, and hundreds or thousands of viewers study along. Channels like TheStrive Studies, MDprospect, and James Scholz have collectively racked up hundreds of millions of views. The format is almost absurdly minimal. A person. A desk. A timer. Yet it works.

Why? The answer lies in a concept psychologists call **body-doubling**: the practice of working alongside another person to increase focus and accountability. It's not tutoring. It's not collaboration. It's simply *presence*.

Body-doubling has gained particular attention in ADHD research. A 2020 study published in the *Journal of Attention Disorders* found that external accountability structures significantly improved task initiation and sustained attention in adults with ADHD. Dr. Russell Barkley, one of the leading researchers in ADHD, has long argued that the disorder is fundamentally about self-regulation — and that external scaffolding (including the presence of others) can compensate for executive function deficits. Body-doubling provides exactly that scaffolding.

But you don't need an ADHD diagnosis to benefit. The phenomenon taps into something universal: **social facilitation**, first described by psychologist Norman Triplett in 1898. We perform better on familiar tasks when others are present. A study room full of focused people makes you more focused. A livestream of someone studying does the same thing, even through a screen.

## From Passive Viewing to Active Participation

The "study with me" model has a limitation, though. You're watching someone else's timer. You're on someone else's schedule. There's no feedback loop — no one knows if you actually studied or scrolled Twitter the whole time. The accountability is one-directional.

We wanted to fix that. **RMH Study** takes the core insight of body-doubling and the Pomodoro Technique and turns it into something interactive, social, and genuinely accountable.

Here's how it works:

- **Synced Pomodoro timers.** When you join a study room, everyone is on the same clock. Work periods and break periods are synchronized, creating a shared rhythm. You can see who's in their work phase and who's on break.
- **Persistent rooms.** You can create or join rooms organized by topic, subject, or just vibe. Want a room for "CS finals prep"? Make one. Want a room that's just "quiet coworking"? It's there.
- **Session leaderboards.** We track total focused minutes per session and per week. This isn't about competition for its own sake — it's about making your effort *visible*. When you can see that you logged 3 hours of focused work today, it reinforces the habit. When you see others logging similar hours, it normalizes the effort.
- **Break activities.** During break periods, we surface light interactive content — a quick typing challenge from RMH Type, a trivia question, a stretch reminder. The goal is to make breaks restorative without letting them spiral into 45-minute YouTube detours.

## Why a Game Studio Built a Productivity App

This is a fair question. We make games — party games, rhythm games, idle clickers, roguelikes. Why build a study timer?

The honest answer: **because our community asked for it.**

We noticed that a significant portion of the people using RMHbox and our Discord daily games were students. They'd play a round of RMHdle in the morning, grind some Temple of Joy during study breaks, and hang out in our Discord voice channels while doing homework. They were already using our ecosystem as a social backdrop for productivity.

RMH Study was a natural extension. We already had the infrastructure — real-time WebSocket connections, user accounts, room management, leaderboard systems. The technical lift was modest. The impact was not.

Since launching the beta, we've seen users log tens of thousands of focused minutes. More importantly, we've seen **retention patterns that look nothing like a game**. People come back daily, not because of dopamine loops or loot boxes, but because they have a calculus exam on Friday and their study group is meeting in Room 4 at 7pm.

## The Design Philosophy

We made deliberate choices to keep RMH Study honest:

- **No gamification traps.** There are no streaks that punish you for missing a day. No premium tiers that gate basic functionality. No dark patterns that guilt you into opening the app. We've seen what happens when productivity tools adopt the worst habits of mobile gaming, and we want no part of it.
- **Transparency about data.** We show you your own stats and we show aggregate, anonymized room stats. We don't sell your study habits to anyone. We don't build advertising profiles. This is a tool, not a surveillance product.
- **Respect for the science.** The default timer is 25/5 because that's what Cirillo's research supports. You can customize it — some people prefer 50/10, others like 15/3 — but we don't let you set a 4-hour work period because the research is clear that ultra-long focus sessions produce diminishing returns and increased burnout.

## What We've Learned

Building RMH Study taught us something we've carried back into our game development: **the most powerful multiplayer experiences aren't always competitive**. Sometimes they're cooperative. Sometimes they're just *parallel* — people doing their own thing, together.

Body-doubling works because it acknowledges a basic truth about human psychology: we are social animals, and our capacity for focus is shaped by our environment. A "study with me" stream provides a weak version of that environment. A synchronized, interactive study room provides a strong one.

The Pomodoro Technique is nearly 40 years old. "Study with me" streams are a decade-old phenomenon. Body-doubling has decades of clinical support behind it. None of this is new. What's new is putting it all together in a real-time, browser-based experience that requires no downloads, no installations, and no subscriptions.

We built RMH Study because we believe the web is the right platform for tools like this — **instant, accessible, social by default**. And because, frankly, we needed it ourselves. Every blog post you read on this site, every game we ship, every feature we debug — there's a good chance it happened during a Pomodoro session in one of our own rooms.

If you want to try it, just open a browser. That's the whole setup process. We think Francesco Cirillo would approve.

title: Sneak Peek: Project Vega
description: A glimpse into the future of our game engine. Something big is coming.
content:
# Sneak Peek: Project Vega

While we've been busy securing the platform, our R&D division has been hard at work on the next generation of our game engine, codenamed: **Project Vega**.

## The Vision

We're not just iterating; we're reimagining. Project Vega aims to bring:
- **Seamless Multiplayer**: Real-time interaction at scale.
- **Enhanced Physics**: More realistic interactions in our 2D spaces.
- **Community Tools**: Built-in support for user-created content.

## Stay Tuned

We're still in the early stages, but the results so far are promising. We'll be sharing more technical deep dives here on the blog soon. 

Make sure your account is set up and verified so you can be among the first to test the beta when it drops.

*End Transmission.*

title: Future Visions: The Upcoming UI of Project Vega
description: A detailed look at the UI redesign process for Future Visions: The Upcoming UI of Project Vega.
content:
UI design is never finished. It evolves. For Future Visions: The Upcoming UI of Project Vega, our goal was to bridge the gap between functionality and aesthetic brilliance. In this post, we'll dive deep into the specific challenges we faced and the solutions we implemented.

## The Objective

The primary goal of the Future Visions: The Upcoming UI of Project Vega redesign was to enhance user engagement while maintaining technical efficiency. We wanted users to feel a sense of fluid interaction, where every click or hover provided meaningful feedback. This required a fundamental rethink of our existing components and spiritual alignment with the overall rmhstudios brand.

We started by auditing our current implementation. What was working? What felt clunky? What was redundant? By stripping back the unnecessary elements, we were able to focus on the core user journey. For Future Visions: The Upcoming UI of Project Vega, this meant focusing on clarity and speed.

## Iterative Design Process

Design at rmhstudios is iterative. We don't just build once and ship. We prototype, test, and refine. For this particular update, we went through three major stages of development:

1.  **Conceptual Wireframing**: Mapping out the raw data flow and user interaction points. We ignored visuals at this stage to focus purely on UX.
2.  **Visual Prototyping**: Applying our signature neon aesthetic. This involved heavy use of CSS variables to ensure consistency across light and dark modes.
3.  **Technical Integration**: Merging the design with our Next.js and Tailwind architecture. Performance was a key metric here—we couldn't let visual polish compromise load times.

One specific challenge was handling responsive layout shifts. As screens get smaller, information density needs to be managed carefully. We implemented custom media queries and flexible grid systems to ensure that Future Visions: The Upcoming UI of Project Vega looks stunning on everything from a widescreen monitor to a handheld device.

## Key Technical Details

From a technical perspective, we relied heavily on modern CSS features. We used `clamp()` for fluid typography and `grid-template-areas` for complex layouts. This allowed us to write cleaner code that is easier to maintain. We also leaned into the power of Framer Motion for micro-animations. These subtle movements—like a button gently pulsing or a card sliding into view—add a layer of polish that makes the web feel alive.

We also prioritized accessibility. Every redesign is an opportunity to improve. We audited our color contrast ratios and ensured that all interactive elements are keyboard-navigable. A beautiful UI is useless if it's not inclusive.

## The Impact

Since implementing these changes, we've seen a noticeable improvement in user retention and satisfaction. The feedback has been overwhelmingly positive. Users appreciate the cleaner aesthetic and the more intuitive navigation. It's a testament to the idea that small, thoughtful changes can have a massive impact on the overall experience.

We are particularly proud of the performance gains. By optimizing our asset delivery and refining our component logic, we've managed to reduce the cumulative layout shift (CLS) and improve the largest contentful paint (LCP) across the site.

## Looking Forward

This is just the beginning. As we move closer to the launch of Project Vega and further updates to our existing games, we'll continue to push the boundaries of what's possible in web-based game UI. We're already experimenting with more advanced shader effects and interactive 3D elements that sit directly in the DOM.

The journey of design is continuous. We're always learning, always growing, and always looking for ways to make rmhstudios.com the best it can be. Stay tuned for more updates as we continue to refine our vision.

## Conclusion

The redesign of Future Visions: The Upcoming UI of Project Vega represents a significant milestone in our design evolution. It's not just about making things look "pretty"; it's about creating an experience that is functional, accessible, and delightful. We hope this deep dive into our process provides some insight into how we approach UI at rmhstudios.

What are your thoughts on this new direction? Do you have suggestions for future redesigns? We'd love to hear from you in the community Discord or via our feedback forms. Your input helps shape the future of this studio.

Thanks for reading, and keep on creating!

title: Rate Limiting and Anti-Cheat in Browser Games — The Honest Approach
description: Browser games can't hide their code, so we built our anti-cheat philosophy around transparency and server authority.
content:
## The Arms Race

Anti-cheat in gaming has become an arms race, and like most arms races, the escalation has produced collateral damage.

**Riot Games' Vanguard**, the kernel-level anti-cheat for Valorant, runs at the highest privilege level of your operating system — Ring 0, the same level as your device drivers. It loads at boot, before you even open the game, and monitors system activity continuously. Riot argues this is necessary to detect sophisticated cheats that operate at the kernel level.

**Easy Anti-Cheat (EAC)** and **BattlEye**, the two most widely deployed third-party anti-cheat solutions, also operate with elevated system privileges, scanning running processes, loaded DLLs, and memory regions for known cheat signatures.

The security community has raised legitimate concerns about this approach. Kernel-level anti-cheat represents a significant attack surface — any vulnerability in the anti-cheat driver is a vulnerability in the operating system itself. In 2023, researchers demonstrated proof-of-concept attacks exploiting anti-cheat drivers, and multiple EAC and BattlEye vulnerabilities have been publicly disclosed over the years.

Beyond security, there's a philosophical issue: **players are being asked to grant game companies the highest level of system access to play a video game.** For many players, especially those with security expertise, this tradeoff feels disproportionate.

## Browser Games: A Different Threat Model

Browser games face a fundamentally different cheating landscape, and it starts with one unavoidable reality: **the client-side code is visible**.

In a native game, the executable is compiled to machine code. Reverse engineering it requires disassemblers, debuggers, and significant expertise. In a browser game, the game logic is JavaScript — viewable in the browser's DevTools with a right-click. Even minified and obfuscated JavaScript can be read and modified by anyone with moderate technical skill.

This means that many traditional anti-cheat strategies simply don't apply:

- **Code obfuscation** slows down casual inspection but doesn't stop determined cheaters. JavaScript obfuscation tools (Terser, javascript-obfuscator) make code harder to read, but the runtime behavior is still inspectable through breakpoints and console logging.
- **Client-side validation** can be bypassed. Any check that runs in the browser can be disabled by modifying the JavaScript. If the client says "this score is legitimate," the client can be made to lie.
- **Memory scanning** isn't possible. Browsers sandbox JavaScript execution and don't expose process memory to web applications. This prevents both cheats and anti-cheat from operating at the memory level.

For some studios, this leads to despair: "browser games can't be secured." We disagree. They just need a different philosophy.

## Server-Authoritative Architecture

The foundation of our anti-cheat strategy is a principle borrowed from networked game design: **the server is the authority on game state**.

In practice, this means:

### The server validates every meaningful action

When a player in RMH Type submits a typing speed, the server doesn't just record the number the client reports. The server has the original text, the timestamps of each keystroke (sent via WebSocket), and the rules for what constitutes a valid input sequence. It independently calculates the words-per-minute from the raw keystroke data.

When a player in RMHbox scores a point in a minigame, the server has the game state, the physics simulation (or a simplified model of it), and the rules for scoring. The client's reported score is a hint, not a verdict.

### The server enforces rate limits

Rate limiting isn't just for API endpoints — it's a critical anti-cheat tool. If a player's client reports 200 actions per second in a game where human input maxes out at roughly 15 actions per second, something is wrong. Our game servers enforce per-player rate limits on:

- Input frequency (keypresses, clicks, movement commands)
- State change requests (item pickups, ability activations)
- Network message volume (total WebSocket messages per second)

Rate limits are set with generous margins above humanly possible input rates, so legitimate players are never affected. But automated scripts and modified clients that flood the server with inputs are caught immediately.

### The server runs authoritative simulations

For competitive games, the server runs its own simulation of the game state. Client inputs are fed into the server simulation, and the server's state is canonical. If the client and server disagree — for example, if the client claims the player is at position X but the server's simulation has them at position Y — the server's state wins, and the client is corrected.

This is computationally expensive. Running server-side simulations for every active game session requires significant server resources. We accept this cost because the alternative — trusting the client — makes competitive integrity impossible.

## What We Don't Do

We're equally deliberate about what we *don't* do:

### We don't install anything on your system

Our games run in a browser tab. They don't install kernel drivers, background processes, or persistent monitoring software. When you close the tab, the game is gone. This is a fundamental advantage of the browser as a gaming platform, and we consider it non-negotiable.

### We don't use fingerprinting for tracking

Browser fingerprinting — collecting device characteristics (screen resolution, installed fonts, GPU model, etc.) to create a unique identifier — is a technique used by some anti-cheat systems to identify banned users who create new accounts. We don't do this because we believe fingerprinting is a privacy violation. We use standard authentication (accounts, session tokens) and accept that determined evaders will create new accounts.

### We don't pretend cheating is impossible

Here's the honest part: **we cannot prevent all cheating in browser games.** A sufficiently motivated cheater can modify the client-side code, automate inputs, or reverse-engineer our network protocol. Our server-authoritative architecture catches the vast majority of cheating attempts, but we can't claim 100% coverage.

We think this honesty is important because the alternative — claiming your anti-cheat is impenetrable — is a lie that erodes trust when it's inevitably disproven.

## The Practical Toolkit

Beyond server authority, we use several practical techniques:

- **Statistical anomaly detection.** We log gameplay metrics (reaction times, accuracy patterns, score distributions) and flag statistical outliers for review. A typing speed of 250 WPM on RMH Type isn't impossible (the world record is around 300 WPM for short bursts), but it's unusual enough to warrant verification.

- **Replay validation.** For leaderboard-eligible scores, we store the complete input sequence and replay it server-side to verify that the inputs produce the claimed result. This is where our deterministic physics engine (discussed in our Laundry Sort physics post) pays dividends — deterministic simulation means replays are exact.

- **Timing analysis.** Human input has characteristic timing patterns — variable intervals between actions, reaction time distributions that follow known curves. Automated inputs tend to have unnaturally consistent timing. We analyze input timing patterns as one signal (not a sole determinant) in our cheat detection.

- **Graceful degradation.** When we detect suspected cheating, we don't ban immediately. We remove the player's scores from public leaderboards and flag the account for review. False positives happen, and permanent bans based on automated detection alone are unjust.

## The Transparency Principle

We publish our approach to anti-cheat (you're reading it) because we believe **security through obscurity is a weak foundation**. Our server-authoritative architecture doesn't depend on cheaters not knowing how it works. It depends on the mathematical reality that the server has information the client doesn't, and the server's validation logic runs in an environment the cheater can't modify.

This transparency serves several purposes:

- **It sets expectations.** Players know that leaderboards are server-validated, that scores are auditable, and that we take competitive integrity seriously.
- **It builds trust.** When we say a leaderboard is fair, players can understand *why* we believe that, not just take our word for it.
- **It invites scrutiny.** If our approach has weaknesses, we'd rather have the community identify them than discover them through exploitation.

## The Broader Lesson

The kernel-level anti-cheat trend in gaming reflects an industry that has chosen escalation over architecture. Rather than designing systems where the server is authoritative, studios have tried to make the client tamper-proof — a goal that is theoretically impossible on hardware the player owns.

Browser games, by their nature, can't pursue the escalation path. The client is JavaScript. It's readable. It's modifiable. This constraint, which might seem like a weakness, is actually a forcing function for better architecture: **if you can't trust the client, you build systems that don't need to.**

That's the approach we've taken. It's not perfect. No anti-cheat is. But it's honest, it respects our players' devices and privacy, and it produces competitive environments that are fair for the overwhelming majority of players.

That's the best we can do, and we think it's enough.

title: Making Games Responsive From Mobile to Ultrawide
description: How RMH Studios adapts game controls, layouts, and experiences across every screen size.
content:
## One Game, Every Screen

A player opens RMHbox on their iPhone during a lunch break. Another joins the same game on a 34-inch ultrawide monitor at home. A third plays on a budget Android tablet. They're all in the same room, playing the same game, seeing interfaces designed specifically for their device.

This is the promise of browser-based gaming, and it's one of the hardest engineering problems we solve at RMH Studios. Responsive web design for content sites is well-understood — text reflows, images resize, layouts stack. Responsive **game** design is a different beast entirely. Game elements have spatial relationships that matter. Controls must map to fundamentally different input methods. Performance budgets vary by orders of magnitude.

This is how we make it work.

## The Mobile-First Reality

The data is unambiguous: **mobile dominates web traffic**. According to StatCounter's GlobalStats, mobile devices accounted for approximately 59% of worldwide web traffic in 2024, a figure that has grown steadily from under 50% in 2017. In many regions — particularly Asia, Africa, and South America — mobile's share exceeds 70%.

For a browser-based game studio, ignoring mobile means ignoring the majority of potential players. But "supporting" mobile isn't enough — mobile must be a **first-class experience**. Players can tell when a game was designed for desktop and grudgingly adapted for phones. The touch targets are too small. The text is too dense. The controls feel like an afterthought.

At RMH Studios, we design mobile-first. Every game begins its design process on a 375-pixel-wide viewport (the width of an iPhone SE). Desktop layouts are the expansion, not the other way around. This discipline ensures that the core experience is always optimized for the most constrained environment.

## The Three Layers of Responsive Gaming

We think about responsive game design in three layers, each addressing a different aspect of the adaptation challenge:

### Layer 1: Layout Responsiveness

This is the closest to traditional responsive web design. Game UI elements — menus, scoreboards, inventory panels, chat windows — need to reposition and resize based on viewport dimensions.

We use a combination of CSS Grid, Flexbox, and CSS `clamp()` functions to create fluid layouts. Key principles:

- **No fixed pixel sizes** for UI elements. Everything scales relative to viewport dimensions or root font size.
- **Breakpoint-aware composition.** At narrow viewports, UI elements stack vertically. At wider viewports, they arrange side-by-side. At ultrawide, additional information panels appear.
- **Safe areas.** Mobile devices with notches, rounded corners, and gesture bars require `env(safe-area-inset-*)` handling to prevent game elements from being obscured.

### Layer 2: Control Responsiveness

This is where game responsiveness diverges from web responsiveness. A button on a website works the same with a mouse click or a finger tap. A game control does not.

**Touch vs. mouse** is the fundamental divide. Mouse input provides:

- Hover states (which touch cannot replicate)
- Precise positioning (pixel-level accuracy)
- Right-click context (secondary actions)
- Scroll wheel (zoom or scroll)

Touch input provides:

- Multi-touch (multiple simultaneous inputs)
- Gestures (swipe, pinch, rotate)
- Device motion (accelerometer, gyroscope)
- Haptic feedback (vibration)

Each RMH game implements **dual control schemes** that are designed independently for each input method, not adapted from one to the other:

In **Neon Driftway**, desktop players steer with arrow keys and drift with spacebar. Mobile players tilt their device to steer and tap to drift. These aren't mappings of the same control — they're different physical interactions designed to feel natural on their respective devices.

In **Laundry Sort**, desktop players click and drag with a mouse. Mobile players use touch-and-fling gestures with velocity calculated from finger movement. The throw physics are tuned differently for each input method because the ergonomics of mouse dragging and finger flicking are fundamentally different.

In **Signal Forge**, desktop players get a card spread visible at all times with hover previews. Mobile players get a scrollable hand with tap-to-preview. The strategic information is identical; the interaction model is completely different.

### Layer 3: Experience Responsiveness

The deepest layer of responsive game design is adapting the **experience itself** to the device's capabilities. This goes beyond layout and controls into the game's visual complexity, information density, and even gameplay parameters.

**Visual scaling:**

- Desktop with dedicated GPU: full particle effects, bloom, shadows, high-resolution assets
- Mobile flagship: reduced particles, simplified post-processing, medium-resolution assets
- Mobile budget: minimal effects, no post-processing, low-resolution assets with crisp UI overlays

We detect device capability through a combination of `navigator.hardwareConcurrency`, `navigator.deviceMemory` (where available), and a brief GPU benchmark that runs during the loading screen. Players are silently categorized into performance tiers, and visual complexity adjusts accordingly.

**Information density:**

Ultrawide monitors can display a game plus a real-time leaderboard, a chat panel, and a minimap simultaneously. A phone screen can display the game and maybe a compact score overlay. We don't remove information on smaller screens — we prioritize it. The most critical information is always visible. Secondary information is accessible through swipes, tabs, or expandable panels.

**Gameplay parameters:**

In some cases, we adjust gameplay itself based on the device. In RMH Type (our competitive typing platform), mobile players use a on-screen keyboard and compete in mobile-specific lobbies with adjusted WPM benchmarks. Desktop players with physical keyboards compete in separate lobbies. The competition is fair within each context.

## Aspect Ratio Challenges

Modern screens range from 9:21 (tall phone) to 32:9 (super ultrawide monitor). That's a variation of over 6x in aspect ratio. For a game with spatial gameplay, this variation creates real design challenges:

**Problem: Viewport advantage.** In a competitive game, a player with a wider screen sees more of the game world. This creates an unfair advantage.

**Solution: Normalized game views.** For competitive games, we define a **canonical viewport** — the rectangle of game world that every player sees regardless of screen shape. On wider screens, the extra width shows non-gameplay elements (decorative borders, expanded UI) rather than more game world. On taller screens, the extra height is used similarly.

**Problem: Camera framing.** In Neon Driftway, the camera follows behind the car. On a 16:9 screen, this framing looks cinematic. On a 9:19 phone held vertically, the same camera angle shows mostly road with the car at the bottom.

**Solution: Per-orientation camera systems.** Neon Driftway detects device orientation and adjusts the camera. Landscape mode uses the standard behind-car view. Portrait mode shifts to a higher, more top-down angle that shows more road ahead, compensating for the narrow horizontal viewport.

## Touch Target Standards

Our minimum touch target size is **44x44 CSS pixels**, following Apple's Human Interface Guidelines. For game-critical controls (the elements you tap rapidly during gameplay), we increase this to **56x56 CSS pixels** minimum, with additional hit-area padding that extends the tappable region beyond the visible element.

This sounds like a small detail, but it's one of the most impactful responsive design decisions we make. A button that's easy to tap on a 6.7-inch iPhone Pro Max is difficult on a 5.4-inch iPhone Mini. Touch targets that seem generous on a tablet are frustratingly small on a phone held in one hand.

We test every game on a range of physical devices — not just screen sizes in a simulator, but actual phones held in actual hands. Simulator testing catches layout issues. Physical device testing catches ergonomic issues.

## The Orientation Question

Should mobile games force landscape? Allow both? Follow device orientation?

Our answer: **it depends on the game**.

- **Neon Driftway** and **Slice It!** strongly prefer landscape. The gameplay is inherently horizontal, and portrait mode would compromise the experience. We display a rotation prompt if the device is in portrait.
- **RMH Type** and **Temple of Joy** work in both orientations. The UI adapts fluidly, and the gameplay is orientation-agnostic.
- **RMHbox** minigames vary. Some are portrait-optimized (they're played on a phone held in one hand). Some are landscape-optimized. The room system communicates the preferred orientation before each minigame starts.

We never lock orientation at the OS level (using `screen.orientation.lock()`), because this feels invasive. Instead, we recommend orientation through UI cues and design the fallback experience to be functional, if not optimal.

## Progressive Enhancement for Gaming

Traditional web development uses progressive enhancement: start with basic HTML, layer on CSS, then add JavaScript. We apply the same philosophy to games:

**Base layer:** The game is playable with minimal visuals, basic controls, and core gameplay. This works on virtually any modern browser.

**Enhanced layer:** On capable devices, we add visual effects, richer audio, haptic feedback, and expanded UI. These enhance the experience but aren't required for it.

**Premium layer:** On high-end devices, we enable maximum visual quality, 120fps rendering (where supported), spatial audio, and features like gamepad support or ultra-wide layouts.

Each player gets the best experience their device can support, automatically and without configuration. That's the goal.

## The Responsive Future

The device landscape is only getting more diverse. Foldable phones create dynamic aspect ratios that change during gameplay. AR glasses will eventually run web content. Car displays, smart TVs, and refrigerator screens (yes, really) all have web browsers.

We can't predict every future form factor, but we can build systems that adapt to constraints rather than targeting specific devices. Our responsive architecture is built on ratios, not pixels. On capabilities, not device names. On the assumption that the next screen will be one we haven't designed for yet.

Browser games should work everywhere a browser works. That's not a marketing promise — it's an engineering commitment. And it's one we renew with every game we ship.

title: Responsive Scaling: How we Handle Various Aspect Ratios
description: A detailed look at the UI redesign process for Responsive Scaling: How we Handle Various Aspect Ratios.
content:
UI design is never finished. It evolves. For Responsive Scaling: How we Handle Various Aspect Ratios, our goal was to bridge the gap between functionality and aesthetic brilliance. In this post, we'll dive deep into the specific challenges we faced and the solutions we implemented.

## The Objective

The primary goal of the Responsive Scaling: How we Handle Various Aspect Ratios redesign was to enhance user engagement while maintaining technical efficiency. We wanted users to feel a sense of fluid interaction, where every click or hover provided meaningful feedback. This required a fundamental rethink of our existing components and spiritual alignment with the overall rmhstudios brand.

We started by auditing our current implementation. What was working? What felt clunky? What was redundant? By stripping back the unnecessary elements, we were able to focus on the core user journey. For Responsive Scaling: How we Handle Various Aspect Ratios, this meant focusing on clarity and speed.

## Iterative Design Process

Design at rmhstudios is iterative. We don't just build once and ship. We prototype, test, and refine. For this particular update, we went through three major stages of development:

1.  **Conceptual Wireframing**: Mapping out the raw data flow and user interaction points. We ignored visuals at this stage to focus purely on UX.
2.  **Visual Prototyping**: Applying our signature neon aesthetic. This involved heavy use of CSS variables to ensure consistency across light and dark modes.
3.  **Technical Integration**: Merging the design with our Next.js and Tailwind architecture. Performance was a key metric here—we couldn't let visual polish compromise load times.

One specific challenge was handling responsive layout shifts. As screens get smaller, information density needs to be managed carefully. We implemented custom media queries and flexible grid systems to ensure that Responsive Scaling: How we Handle Various Aspect Ratios looks stunning on everything from a widescreen monitor to a handheld device.

This allowed us to write cleaner code that is easier to maintain. We also leaned into the power of Framer Motion for micro-animations. We also prioritized accessibility. Every redesign is an opportunity to improve. We audited our color contrast ratios and ensured that all interactive elements are keyboard-navigable. A beautiful UI is useless if it's not inclusive.

## The Impact

Since implementing these changes, we've seen a noticeable improvement in user retention and satisfaction. The feedback has been overwhelmingly positive. Users appreciate the cleaner aesthetic and the more intuitive navigation. It's a testament to the idea that small, thoughtful changes can have a massive impact on the overall experience.

We are particularly proud of the performance gains. By optimizing our asset delivery and refining our component logic, we've managed to reduce the cumulative layout shift (CLS) and improve the largest contentful paint (LCP) across the site.

## Looking Forward

This is just the beginning. As we move closer to the launch of Project Vega and further updates to our existing games, we'll continue to push the boundaries of what's possible in web-based game UI. We're already experimenting with more advanced shader effects and interactive 3D elements that sit directly in the DOM.

The journey of design is continuous. We're always learning, always growing, and always looking for ways to make rmhstudios.com the best it can be. Stay tuned for more updates as we continue to refine our vision.

## Conclusion

The redesign of Responsive Scaling: How we Handle Various Aspect Ratios represents a significant milestone in our design evolution. It's not just about making things look "pretty"; it's about creating an experience that is functional, accessible, and delightful. We hope this deep dive into our process provides some insight into how we approach UI at rmhstudios.

What are your thoughts on this new direction? Do you have suggestions for future redesigns? We'd love to hear from you in the community Discord or via our feedback forms. Your input helps shape the future of this studio.

Thanks for reading, and keep on creating!

title: RMH Studios 2026 Roadmap — What's Coming
description: Our ambitious but honest plan for 2026 — new games, platform features, and the infrastructure to support them.
content:
## Building on Momentum

2025 was the year we proved that RMH Studios could ship. 2026 is the year we prove we can scale. Not scale in the Silicon Valley "growth at all costs" sense — scale in the sense of building deeper, richer experiences while maintaining the quality and honesty our community expects.

This roadmap is ambitious. It's also honest. We'll flag where we're confident, where we're exploring, and where we might be wrong. Roadmaps that only contain certainties aren't roadmaps — they're press releases.

## Games: Expanding What Works

### RMHbox: 25+ Minigames by Year End

RMHbox is our flagship, and we're doubling down on it. The target is to reach **25 or more minigames** in the catalog by the end of 2026, up from 16+ today.

But quantity isn't the goal — variety is. We're specifically targeting gaps in our current lineup:

- **Collaborative games** where the whole room works together instead of competing. Our player feedback consistently asks for co-op experiences.
- **Creative games** where players draw, write, or build things. These generate the most memorable moments and the most shareable content.
- **Physical games** that use device sensors — accelerometer, gyroscope — on mobile devices, turning phones into motion controllers for party play.

We're also building a **community minigame submission pipeline**. Players with game ideas will be able to pitch concepts, and selected ideas will be developed with credit to the creator. This isn't just community engagement — it's a recognition that our players have great ideas we'd never think of.

### Signal Forge: Full Campaign

Signal Forge's core deckbuilding loop is solid. In 2026, we're wrapping it in a full campaign structure:

- **Three acts** with distinct environments, enemy types, and mechanical themes.
- **A meta-progression system** where completed runs unlock new cards, characters, and modifiers for future runs. The roguelike loop needs long-term incentive structures, and we're building them.
- **Weekly challenge runs** with fixed seeds and community leaderboards. This gives competitive players a reason to replay and optimizers a canvas to work on.

### Echoes of the Spire: The Expansion

Echoes of the Spire's narrative extraction format resonated with players in ways we didn't expect. The expansion will add:

- **Two new narrative arcs**, each with their own set of documents, audio logs, and environmental storytelling.
- **Branching investigation paths** that change based on which evidence you prioritize.
- **Community theories board** — an in-game space where players can post and vote on theories about the story, creating a collaborative investigation layer.

### New Game Announcements

We have two new games in active development. We're not ready to announce details, but we'll share this much:

- One is a **multiplayer strategy game** that explores asynchronous play — think turns that happen over hours, not seconds. It's designed for people who want a game that fits into their day rather than demanding their full attention.
- The other is a **creative sandbox** that leverages WebGPU for real-time 3D rendering in the browser. This is our most technically ambitious project to date, and it's the testbed for our next-generation graphics pipeline.

We'll announce both properly when they're ready to show, with real gameplay footage — no mock-ups, no target renders, no lies.

## Platform: The Connective Tissue

Individual games are products. A platform is an ecosystem. In 2026, we're building the infrastructure that connects everything.

### Accounts and Profiles

This is the biggest platform investment of the year. We're building an **RMH account system** that spans all our games and tools:

- A single identity across RMHbox, Signal Forge, RMHTube, RMH Type, RMH Study, and all future products.
- **Player profiles** showing game history, achievements, and community contributions.
- **Friend lists** that work across games — see what your friends are playing and join them with one click.

We've deliberately delayed building accounts because we wanted to get it right. Account systems are trust infrastructure. They handle personal data. They need to be secure, private, and transparent about what data we collect and why. We're not bolting on auth as an afterthought — we're building it as a first-class platform feature.

### Cross-Game Progression

With accounts in place, we can build **cross-game rewards**:

- Daily game streaks in RMHdle unlock cosmetics in RMHbox.
- Signal Forge achievements unlock profile badges visible across all games.
- RMH Type milestones (words per minute records, total words typed) contribute to a global player level.

The goal is to make every game feel like part of a larger universe without forcing players to engage with games they're not interested in. Opt-in connection, not mandatory grinding.

### Improved Multiplayer Infrastructure

Our WebSocket infrastructure works, but 2026 demands we make it excellent:

- **Regional server nodes** to reduce latency for players outside our current server region.
- **Spectator mode** for RMHbox games, letting players watch ongoing rounds before joining.
- **Room persistence** so that game rooms can survive brief disconnections and browser refreshes without losing state.

## Technology: Betting on the Future

### WebGPU Adoption

As we discussed in our recent WebGPU post, 2026 is the year we start building on WebGPU in production. Our plan:

- Migrate Three.js rendering to the `WebGPURenderer` for supported browsers, with automatic WebGL fallback.
- Use compute shaders for particle effects, physics, and procedural generation in our new 3D sandbox game.
- Build a performance comparison dashboard to share real-world WebGPU vs. WebGL metrics with the community.

### Progressive Web App Improvements

We're investing in PWA capabilities to blur the line between browser game and native app:

- **Install prompts** for players who want our games on their home screen.
- **Offline mode** for single-player experiences like Signal Forge (using service workers to cache game state).
- **Push notifications** for daily game reminders, friend invites, and event announcements (opt-in only, obviously).

### Performance Budget System

We're implementing a formal performance budget for every game: maximum initial bundle size, maximum time-to-interactive, minimum frame rate on target hardware. If a feature pushes a game over budget, the feature gets optimized or cut. No exceptions.

## Industry Trends Shaping Our Plan

Several industry developments influence our 2026 strategy:

**The browser as gaming platform is gaining legitimacy.** Netflix Games has been exploring browser-based game delivery. Google's web gaming investments continue. The conversation has shifted from "can the browser run games?" to "what kinds of games run best in the browser?"

**AI tools are accelerating development.** We're using AI-assisted development for code generation, asset creation, and testing. This doesn't replace human creativity and judgment — it amplifies our small team's output. We expect AI tooling to be a significant multiplier for indie studios in 2026.

**Community-driven development is the new normal.** Early access, public roadmaps, transparent communication — these aren't novel anymore. Players expect to be partners in development, not just customers. Our community-first approach, which felt unusual when we started, is becoming industry standard.

**WebGPU reaches critical mass.** With Chrome and Safari supporting WebGPU and Firefox closing the gap, 2026 is the year browser games can realistically target modern GPU features without alienating large portions of the audience.

## What We're Not Doing

Honest roadmaps include what you've decided *against*:

- **We're not building a native app.** The browser is our platform. Wrapping our games in Electron or shipping to app stores would compromise our core thesis.
- **We're not raising venture capital.** VC money comes with growth expectations that conflict with building patiently and honestly. We're bootstrapping.
- **We're not chasing trends.** We won't build a battle royale because they're popular, or add blockchain because it's buzzy. We build what serves our players and our creative vision.

## The Honesty Clause

Some items on this roadmap won't ship in 2026. That's the nature of ambitious plans executed by a small team. When things slip or change, we'll tell you why. When we cut something, we'll explain the reasoning. When we're wrong about a bet, we'll own it.

A roadmap is a promise to try, not a guarantee of delivery. We take that promise seriously.

2026 is going to be big. Let's build it together.

title: Our Commitment to Honesty in Game Development
description: Why RMH Studios chose radical transparency in an industry plagued by broken promises.
content:
## The Trust Problem in Gaming

The gaming industry has a credibility problem. Players have been burned — repeatedly — by studios that prioritize hype over honesty, and the consequences are eroding the relationship between developers and the communities they serve.

Consider the launch of **Cyberpunk 2077** in December 2020. CD Projekt Red, a studio once celebrated for consumer-friendly practices, shipped a game that was borderline unplayable on the consoles it was marketed for. Review copies were restricted to PC only, hiding the console performance issues from press and players alike. The fallout was severe: Sony pulled the game from the PlayStation Store, CD Projekt's stock price cratered, and a class-action lawsuit followed. The trust that studio had built over a decade with *The Witcher 3* evaporated in a week.

Then came September 2023, when **Unity Technologies announced a runtime fee** that would charge developers every time a game using its engine was installed. The backlash was immediate and volcanic. Indie studios that had built their entire businesses on Unity felt betrayed — some had games already shipped, and the retroactive nature of the fee threatened their livelihoods. Unity eventually walked back most of the changes, but the damage was done. Developers learned a hard lesson: the platforms and tools you depend on can change the rules without warning.

These aren't isolated incidents. They're symptoms of a broader pattern where short-term revenue pressure overrides long-term trust.

## Why We Founded RMH Studios on Transparency

When we started RMH Studios, we made a deliberate choice: **we would rather under-promise and over-deliver than chase hype.** That might sound like a platitude, but we've embedded it into how we operate at every level.

Here's what that looks like in practice:

- **We don't announce features until they're working.** If you see us talk about a mechanic in Signal Forge or a new minigame in RMHbox, it exists in a playable state. We've watched too many studios announce ambitious roadmaps only to silently shelve half the items.
- **We share our development process openly.** Our Discord community sees work-in-progress builds, hears about technical challenges we're facing, and gets honest timelines. When something is delayed, we say so — and we say why.
- **We don't manipulate our marketing.** No bullshots, no "target renders" passed off as gameplay, no cherry-picked review quotes stripped of context. What you see on our site is what you get in the browser tab.

## Honesty as a Competitive Advantage

There's a cynical view that honesty is a luxury — that you need hype to compete. We disagree. For an indie studio building browser games, trust is our most valuable currency.

Our players don't pay $60 upfront. They don't commit to a multi-gigabyte download. They click a link and they're playing. That low barrier to entry means we have to earn attention in seconds, but it also means **our players have zero sunk-cost bias.** They'll leave the moment something feels dishonest or manipulative. And they should.

This dynamic forces us to be better. Every claim we make about our games has to hold up the instant someone opens a browser tab. There's no install process to build anticipation, no loading screen slideshow to set expectations. The game *is* the pitch.

## What Honesty Looks Like Day-to-Day

Transparency isn't just a marketing stance — it shapes our engineering and design decisions too.

When we build features, we document our reasoning. When we make tradeoffs (and browser game development is *full* of tradeoffs — performance vs. visual fidelity, latency vs. feature richness), we talk about them publicly. We believe players are smart enough to understand that a browser game running on WebSockets has different constraints than a AAA title with dedicated servers, and we'd rather have that conversation openly than pretend the constraints don't exist.

We also fact-check ourselves rigorously. When we write about our technology stack, we cite what's real. When we compare our approach to industry alternatives, we do our homework. We've seen too many dev blogs make vague claims about "revolutionary" this or "unprecedented" that. We'd rather be specific, accurate, and occasionally boring than exciting and wrong.

### The Bar We Hold Ourselves To

Every piece of public communication from RMH Studios — whether it's a blog post, a Discord announcement, or a game description — goes through a simple filter:

1. **Is this true?** Not "technically true" or "true from a certain point of view." Actually true.
2. **Would we be comfortable if a player quoted this back to us in six months?** If the answer is no, we rewrite it.
3. **Are we being transparent about what we don't know?** Uncertainty is honest. False confidence is not.

## Building Trust in an Era of Skepticism

The gaming audience in 2024 is more skeptical than ever, and rightfully so. Years of misleading trailers, broken launch promises, and exploitative monetization have trained players to assume the worst. Rebuilding that trust — or building it from scratch as a new studio — requires consistency over time.

We're not going to earn trust with a single blog post or a single game launch. We're going to earn it by doing what we say, shipping what we show, and being honest when we fall short. That's the foundation we're building RMH Studios on, and every game we release, from RMHbox's party chaos to Temple of Joy's philosophical depths, carries that commitment.

**We believe the next generation of browser games deserves a studio that tells the truth.** That's what we're here to build.

title: The Vision Behind RMHbox
description: Building a Jackbox-style party game platform in the browser — accessible to anyone with a phone.
content:
## Party Games Shouldn't Require a Console

The last time you played a party game with friends, how many steps did it take to start? If it was a console game, someone needed the right hardware, the right controllers, the right game installed, and enough couch space. If it was Jackbox, someone needed to stream the game while everyone else joined on their phones — which works well, but still requires a purchase and a host device.

We built RMHbox because we believe the answer should be simpler: **open a link, join a room, play**.

RMHbox is our party game platform — a collection of 16+ minigames designed for groups, playable entirely in the browser. No downloads, no installs, no host device required. One player creates a room, shares a code, and everyone joins from whatever device they have. Phones, tablets, laptops — it all works.

This is the post where we explain why we built it, what inspired us, and how 16+ minigames coexist in a single browser-based platform.

## The Jackbox Model — And Where It Stops

**Jackbox Games** pioneered the modern party game format. Starting with *You Don't Know Jack* and evolving into the Party Pack series, they solved the controller problem brilliantly: use phones as controllers, display the game on a shared screen. It's a model that has sold millions of copies and created genuine cultural moments — Quiplash answers becoming inside jokes, Drawful drawings becoming group lore.

We have deep respect for what Jackbox built. Their design philosophy — low barrier to entry, humor-driven gameplay, asymmetric information between the shared screen and personal devices — directly influenced RMHbox.

But the Jackbox model has constraints we wanted to eliminate:

- **Purchase required.** Each Party Pack costs $25-30. That's fair value, but it means someone in the group must own it.
- **Host device required.** One device needs to run the game and display it on a shared screen. This works great in a living room but poorly for remote play without additional streaming setup.
- **Platform-locked.** Jackbox games run on specific platforms — Steam, consoles, Apple TV. You can't just open a browser and play.
- **Fixed game packs.** Each Party Pack is a bundle of five games. You get what's in the box — no additions, no community contributions, no evolving library.

RMHbox removes all four constraints. The platform is free to play, runs entirely in browsers, requires no host device (every player sees what they need to see on their own screen), and the game library grows continuously.

## The Decline of Local Multiplayer

There's a broader trend motivating RMHbox: **local multiplayer is disappearing**. The games industry has shifted overwhelmingly toward online multiplayer, single-player experiences, and live-service models. The percentage of major releases supporting local co-op or competitive play has declined steadily over the past decade.

This is understandable from a business perspective — online multiplayer drives engagement metrics and recurring revenue. But it's a cultural loss. Some of the best gaming memories are made in the same room — the trash talk, the shared laughter, the collective gasps. These moments don't translate well to voice chat over a headset.

RMHbox exists to preserve that energy. Even when players are remote, the games are designed for **synchronous social play** — everyone playing at the same time, reacting to each other, creating shared moments. The games are short, loud, and designed to generate conversation.

## 16+ Minigames, One Platform

The technical challenge of RMHbox isn't any individual game — it's hosting 16+ games in a single platform with shared infrastructure. Here's how the architecture works:

### The Room System

Every RMHbox session starts with a **room**. One player creates a room and receives a four-character code. Other players enter that code to join. The room persists across multiple games — you don't need to reconnect between rounds.

Under the hood, rooms are managed through WebSocket connections. When a player joins, their device establishes a persistent connection to the game server. All game state flows through this connection — inputs, game events, scoring, and transitions.

The room system handles:

- **Player management** — joining, leaving, reconnecting after network drops
- **Game selection** — the room host (or a vote) picks which minigame to play next
- **Score tracking** — cumulative scores across multiple games in a session
- **State synchronization** — ensuring all players see consistent game state

### The Minigame Architecture

Each minigame in RMHbox is a **self-contained module** that plugs into the room system. A minigame defines:

- **Input schema** — what controls the game needs (tapping, drawing, typing, tilting)
- **Game loop** — the core gameplay logic
- **Rendering** — what each player sees on their device
- **Scoring** — how points are calculated
- **Duration** — how long a round lasts

This modular architecture means we can develop and test minigames independently, then plug them into the platform. Adding a new game to RMHbox doesn't require changing the room system, the scoring system, or the session flow. It's a plugin.

### Game Categories

Our 16+ minigames span several categories:

- **Trivia & knowledge** — classic quiz formats with twists
- **Drawing & creativity** — prompts that reward humor over skill
- **Speed & reflex** — tap-fast challenges and reaction games
- **Strategy & deception** — social deduction and bluffing games
- **Word games** — vocabulary and wordplay challenges
- **Physics & chaos** — games that use device motion and touch in unexpected ways

The variety is intentional. Different games appeal to different players, and the rotation keeps sessions fresh. A 45-minute RMHbox session might include a trivia game, a drawing game, a speed challenge, and a deception game — each lasting 3-5 minutes.

## Making It Work on Every Device

The hardest design constraint for RMHbox is **device diversity**. In a single room, one player might be on a 27-inch desktop monitor, another on an iPhone SE, and a third on an Android tablet. The games need to work on all of them.

Our approach is **adaptive UI** — not just responsive layouts, but fundamentally different interaction models based on the device:

- **Touch devices** get tap targets, swipe gestures, and device motion controls
- **Desktop devices** get mouse/keyboard controls with larger visual displays
- **Small screens** get simplified UI with larger text and fewer simultaneous elements
- **Large screens** get richer displays with more game information visible

Each minigame's input schema defines how controls map across device types. A drawing game uses touch on phones and mouse on desktops. A speed game uses taps on phones and key presses on desktops. The game logic is identical — only the input layer adapts.

## The Social Layer

Party games live and die on their social dynamics. The gameplay is the vehicle, but the destination is **laughter, competition, and connection**. We've designed several features specifically to amplify the social experience:

- **Reaction system** — players can send quick emoji reactions during games, visible to everyone
- **Replay moments** — particularly funny or impressive moments are automatically captured and replayed between rounds
- **Custom prompts** — for creative games, players can submit their own prompts, making the experience personal to the group
- **Session history** — after a session ends, players get a recap of highlights, scores, and memorable moments

## Why Free-to-Play

RMHbox is free. No purchase, no subscription, no paywall. This is a deliberate choice rooted in our philosophy about party games.

The value of a party game is directly proportional to how many people can play it. A party game that costs money creates a social friction: someone has to pay, someone has to own it, and the group is limited to what the owner has purchased. By making RMHbox free, we remove that friction entirely. Anyone can host. Anyone can join. The only requirement is a browser.

We sustain development through our broader platform — the games and apps across RMH Studios contribute to a ecosystem where the party game platform serves as a community hub and discovery engine for our other titles.

## What's Ahead

We're currently developing our next wave of minigames, with a focus on **asymmetric gameplay** — games where different players have different roles, information, or objectives. We're also building a **custom game creator** that will let players design and share their own minigames within the RMHbox platform.

The vision for RMHbox is simple but ambitious: **become the default answer to "what should we play tonight?"** No setup, no cost, no barriers. Just a link, a room code, and a group of people ready to laugh together.

The party starts in the browser.

title: The Story Behind RMHTube
description: How a hackathon idea became a full watch party platform — and what we learned about WebSocket sync along the way.
content:
## Watching Together, Apart

The idea was simple: watch a video with friends, in sync, from different locations. No countdown coordination. No "okay, press play... now!" Just a shared player that keeps everyone on the same frame, with a chat alongside for reactions.

RMHTube started as a hackathon project in early 2021, when synchronized watching was still a novelty for many people. By the time we launched it as a full platform, the watch party space had exploded — and we'd learned hard lessons about real-time synchronization, user expectations, and the surprising complexity of "just playing a video at the same time."

This is the story of RMHTube: where it came from, what broke along the way, and what we built to fix it.

## The Pandemic Watch Party Boom

The COVID-19 pandemic fundamentally changed how people consumed media together. With in-person gatherings restricted worldwide, the demand for virtual co-watching experiences surged. Several platforms emerged to fill the gap:

**Teleparty** (formerly Netflix Party) launched in 2020 and rapidly grew to over 10 million users by synchronizing Netflix playback across browsers with a chat sidebar. **Disney+ GroupWatch** launched in September 2020, offering native synchronized viewing for up to seven participants. **Amazon Prime Video Watch Party** followed shortly after. **Scener** positioned itself as a virtual movie theater, supporting multiple streaming services.

These platforms validated the concept — people wanted to watch together online — but each had limitations:

- **Platform lock-in.** Most tools only worked with specific streaming services.
- **Extension dependency.** Many required browser extensions, which limited mobile access.
- **Limited interaction.** Chat was usually text-only, with no reactions, no shared controls, and no integration with other social features.
- **Sync fragility.** Users frequently reported playback falling out of sync, particularly during buffering events or when participants had different internet speeds.
