title: Building a Song Community: User Uploads and Discovery
description: How we built Slice It!'s community ecosystem with user uploads, ratings, comments, and leaderboards.
content:
## A Game Is Only as Good as Its Library

The best rhythm game engine in the world doesn't matter if there's nothing to play. I learned this lesson early in development — the technical side of Slice It! was coming together beautifully, but every time I demoed it to someone, the first question was always: *"Where do I get songs?"*

For Slice It! to thrive, it needed more than a handful of demo tracks. It needed a **living, growing library** powered by the community itself. Players upload music. Other players discover it, play it, rate it, compete on it, and talk about it. The library becomes a destination — a reason to come back every day to see what's new.

Building that ecosystem turned out to be one of the most rewarding parts of the entire project.

## The Upload Flow

Uploading a song to Slice It! is intentionally simple. We wanted the barrier to be as low as possible — if you have an audio file, you should be able to share it with the community in under a minute.

The upload form captures a few key details:

- **Audio file** (MP3, WAV, OGG, FLAC — we support the major formats)
- **Song title and artist name**
- **Genre tags** (selected from a predefined list plus custom tags)
- **Optional album art**

When the upload is submitted, the server processes the audio file in the background. It decodes the audio, runs a quick validation pass to ensure the file isn't corrupted or too short, generates a preview waveform for the library UI, and pre-computes the beatmap so the first player doesn't have to wait.

```typescript
async function processSongUpload(file: Buffer, metadata: SongMetadata) {
  const audioBuffer = await decodeAudio(file);
  
  validateAudio(audioBuffer); // throws if invalid
  
  const waveform = generateWaveformPreview(audioBuffer);
  const beatmap = generateBeatmap(audioBuffer);
  const duration = audioBuffer.duration;
  
  await db.song.create({
    data: {
      title: metadata.title,
      artist: metadata.artist,
      genre: metadata.genre,
      duration,
      waveform,
      beatmapData: beatmap,
      uploadedBy: metadata.userId,
    },
  });
}
```

## The Song Library Interface

The library is where players spend their time when they're not actively playing. It's designed to make discovery effortless and browsing enjoyable.

### Search and Filtering

Players can search by song title, artist, genre, or uploader. Filters narrow results by duration, difficulty, rating, and upload date. The search is fuzzy — typing "beathoven" will still find "Beethoven" — because typos shouldn't stand between a player and a great song.

### Sort Options

The library supports multiple sort modes:

- **Trending** — Songs gaining plays and ratings quickly
- **Top Rated** — Highest community ratings over all time
- **Most Played** — Pure play count ranking
- **Newest** — Most recent uploads first
- **Random Discovery** — A random shuffle for adventurous players

The **Trending** algorithm deserves a brief mention. It's a time-decayed scoring system that weights recent activity more heavily than historical totals. A song uploaded yesterday with 50 plays and a 4.5 rating will rank higher than a song from three months ago with 500 plays and a 4.8 rating. This keeps the library feeling fresh and gives new uploads a fair chance at visibility.

### Song Cards

Each song in the library shows a compact card with the title, artist, duration, difficulty indicator, community rating, play count, and the waveform preview. Hovering over a card plays a short audio preview — just enough to decide if you want to play the full song.

## Ratings and Reviews

After playing a song, players can rate it from 1 to 5 stars. They can also leave a written comment — thoughts on the music, the generated beatmap quality, or just general vibes. These ratings and comments are visible on the song's detail page.

Comments are threaded, allowing conversations between players about specific songs. I've already seen fascinating discussions emerge during playtesting — players sharing strategies for tricky sections, debating whether a particular beatmap captured the song's energy, and recommending similar tracks.

The rating system feeds directly into the library's discovery algorithms. Higher-rated songs surface more prominently, creating a virtuous cycle: good music rises, players find it, they rate it highly, which pushes it higher. It's the flywheel effect that every community platform dreams of.

## Per-Song Leaderboards

Every song in the library has its own **leaderboard**. After completing a song, your score is submitted and ranked against every other player who has played that same track.

Leaderboards show the top 100 scores by default, along with your personal ranking and the rankings of anyone on your friends list. Each entry displays the player's name, score, max combo, accuracy percentage, and the date they set the record.

This creates compelling long-tail engagement. Players don't just play a song once — they come back to improve their ranking. A new high score appearing above yours is a notification, a challenge, a reason to fire up the game and try one more time.

### Score Integrity

For leaderboards to matter, players need to trust them. We implement several measures to prevent cheating:

- **Server-side score validation** that cross-references hit counts and timing against the beatmap
- **Replay data submission** that allows suspicious scores to be reviewed
- **Statistical anomaly detection** that flags scores mathematically unlikely given the song's difficulty

We can't prevent all cheating — no system can — but we can make it difficult enough that the vast majority of leaderboard entries are legitimate.

## Content Moderation

Opening uploads to the community means opening the door to abuse. Copyrighted music, inappropriate content, spam uploads, and low-quality files are all potential problems.

Our moderation approach is multi-layered:

### Automated Checks

- **Audio fingerprinting** to detect known copyrighted tracks (we compare against a database of flagged fingerprints)
- **Duration limits** — songs shorter than 30 seconds or longer than 15 minutes are rejected
- **File size limits** — prevents someone from uploading a 2GB WAV file
- **Metadata validation** — titles and artist fields are checked for profanity and spam patterns

### Community Reporting

Players can report songs for copyright issues, inappropriate content, or quality problems. Reports are reviewed by moderators, and songs that violate community guidelines are removed. Repeat offenders receive upload restrictions.

### Upload Reputation

New accounts have limited upload slots. As players build a track record of quality uploads (reflected by ratings and play counts), their upload quota increases. This naturally throttles spam while rewarding genuine contributors.

## Building a Culture

The technical infrastructure of a community platform is only half the battle. The other half is **culture** — the unwritten norms that determine what the community feels like.

We've been intentional about fostering a positive, encouraging atmosphere. The comment system shows the commenter's play stats for that song (so you know if the person giving feedback has actually played it). Rating is constructive, not punitive. The UI language emphasizes discovery and sharing over competition and gatekeeping.

Early playtesting helped enormously here. The first hundred or so players established the tone — supportive, curious, excited to share music. We've learned to protect that tone, because once it's set, it tends to self-reinforce. New players see existing comments and match the vibe.

## Reflections

Building the community features forced me to think about Slice It! differently. The audio engine and beatmap generator are technical achievements. But the community — the shared library, the ratings, the comments, the leaderboards — that's what turns Slice It! from a *product* into a *place*.

A place where someone uploads a song they love, and a stranger on the other side of the world discovers it, plays it, and leaves a comment saying it made their day. That's the magic I was chasing. And slowly, post by post, song by song, it's happening.

title: The Idea Behind Slice It!: A New Kind of Rhythm Game
description: How Slice It! was born from a love of rhythm games and a frustration with their limitations.
content:
## Where It All Started

Every game begins with a moment of frustration. For me, it was sitting in front of my monitor, tabbing between osu! and Guitar Hero clone after Guitar Hero clone, thinking: *why does every rhythm game make me wait for someone else to create the beatmap?*

I've been a rhythm game fanatic for as long as I can remember. Guitar Hero got me hooked as a kid — the plastic guitar, the colored notes streaming down the highway, the thrill of nailing a solo on Expert. Later, osu! consumed hundreds of hours of my life. The precision, the community, the endless song library. These games shaped how I think about interactive music experiences.

But there was always a gap. A song I loved would have no beatmap. Or the only available map was poorly timed. Or I'd discover an incredible track from an indie artist and realize I'd have to spend hours manually charting it before I could play. That friction between *hearing* a song and *playing* a song felt like a problem worth solving.

## The Core Vision

Slice It! was born from a simple question: **What if the game could listen to any song and generate a playable beatmap automatically?**

Not a gimmick. Not a rough approximation. A genuinely musical, fun-to-play map that responds to the dynamics of the audio in real time. I wanted a system that could take any MP3 or audio file — whether it's a symphonic metal track, a lo-fi beat, or an electronic banger — and produce a note chart that *feels* right.

That meant building an audio analysis engine from scratch. No relying on BPM metadata tags (which are often wrong or missing). No requiring users to manually input tempo information. The game would parse the actual waveform, detect onsets, analyze frequency content, and translate musical events into gameplay moments.

The idea excited me more than anything I'd worked on in years.

## What Makes Slice It! Different

There are a few pillars that I knew from day one would define Slice It! and separate it from the rhythm games I grew up with:

### 1. Automatic Beatmap Generation

This is the headline feature. Drop in any audio file, and the game generates a beatmap. No editor needed (though we may add one later for fine-tuning). The analysis engine handles onset detection, spectral analysis, and dynamic difficulty scaling. It's not perfect — and honestly, achieving "perfect" may be impossible — but it's surprisingly musical, and it gets better with every iteration.

### 2. A Softer Aesthetic

Most rhythm games lean into neon, high-contrast visuals. Flashy particle effects, dark backgrounds, aggressive color palettes. I love that aesthetic, but I wanted Slice It! to feel different. We went with a **neumorphic design system** — soft shadows, rounded corners, muted blues and pinks against white and light gray backgrounds. It's calming. It lets the music be the star. Players have told me it feels like playing a rhythm game inside a cloud, and I think that's the best compliment we've received.

### 3. Community-First Design

From the earliest planning documents, I knew Slice It! needed a public music library. Users upload songs, rate them, leave comments, and compete on per-song leaderboards. The game isn't just a solo experience — it's a living ecosystem of music and competition. Every song someone uploads becomes a new playground for every other player.

### 4. Real-Time Multiplayer

Up to four players can compete simultaneously via WebSocket connections. Lobby codes, shareable links, queued song playlists. The synchronization challenges were enormous (more on that in a future post), but the payoff is incredible. There's nothing quite like going head-to-head with friends on a song none of you have ever played before, with the beatmap generating in real time for everyone.

## Early Days and Doubts

I won't pretend the early days were all excitement. There were moments of serious doubt. Could browser-based audio processing really handle real-time analysis? Would the Web Audio API give me enough control? Was HTML5 Canvas fast enough for a 60fps rhythm game with potentially hundreds of notes on screen?

I spent the first two weeks just prototyping the audio pipeline. Loading files, decoding them, running FFT analysis, and visualizing the results. The early outputs were rough — the onset detection triggered on everything, generating chaotic walls of notes that were impossible to play. But buried in that chaos were moments where the system *got it right*. A snare hit would trigger a note. A bass drop would spawn a cluster. A vocal phrase would create a flowing sequence.

Those moments kept me going.

## The Tech Stack Decision

I chose **Next.js with TypeScript** as the foundation. It might seem unconventional for a game, but the reasoning was practical: Slice It! isn't just a game — it's a platform. User accounts, song uploads, leaderboards, comments, ratings. All of that needs a robust web framework. Next.js gives me server-side rendering for the community pages, API routes for the backend, and a solid React foundation for the UI.

The actual gameplay runs on **HTML5 Canvas**, which sits inside the Next.js app. The canvas handles all the real-time rendering — the note highway, hit animations, score displays — while React manages everything around it: menus, song selection, user profiles, multiplayer lobbies.

TypeScript was non-negotiable. A project this complex, with audio processing, real-time networking, and game state management, would be a debugging nightmare without static types. TypeScript has caught countless bugs before they ever reached a browser.

## Looking Forward

This post is the first in what I hope will be a long series of devlogs. I want to document the journey honestly — the breakthroughs and the setbacks, the clever solutions and the ugly hacks, the moments of joy and the 2 AM debugging sessions.

Slice It! is the most ambitious project I've ever undertaken at RMH Studios. It combines my love of music, my passion for game development, and my belief that the web platform is capable of far more than most people give it credit for.

The road ahead is long, but the first notes are already playing. Let's see where the rhythm takes us.

title: Player Feedback That Shaped Slice It!
description: Real player feedback that changed features, difficulty, and design decisions in Slice It!.
content:
## The Humbling Part

There's a moment every developer dreads and desperately needs: the moment real humans play your game and tell you what they actually think. Not polite encouragement. Not vague "it's cool." The real, unfiltered, sometimes brutal feedback that reveals the gap between what you *think* you built and what players actually experience.

For Slice It!, that moment came during our first structured playtest in early spring. I invited 30 players — a mix of rhythm game veterans, casual gamers, and people who'd never touched a rhythm game in their life. I gave them the game, a feedback form, and asked them to be honest.

They were honest. And it changed everything.

## "I Can't Tell If I'm Hitting Notes or Missing Them"

This was, by far, the most common piece of feedback in the first playtest. Players were pressing keys, seeing *something* happen, but not understanding whether they'd hit the note, missed it, or hit it late.

The problem was our visual feedback system. In the original design, hitting a note produced a small, elegant ripple effect — beautiful, subtle, and completely invisible during intense gameplay. The "Perfect" ripple was slightly blue. The "Good" ripple was slightly pink. The "Miss" was a brief fade. The differences were too subtle, too similar, too easy to miss in the peripheral vision.

We overhauled the feedback system completely:

- **"Perfect" hits** now produce a bright, unmistakable circular burst with the word "PERFECT" flashing briefly above the hit zone
- **"Great" and "Good" hits** produce progressively smaller, dimmer effects with their own text labels
- **Misses** produce a distinctive downward animation — the note visibly falls through the hit zone and shatters
- **Sound effects** accompany each result — a satisfying click for Perfect, a softer tap for Good, and a muted thud for Miss

The hit feedback went from the subtlest element on screen to the most prominent. It felt like a step backward in design elegance, but players immediately responded. Their accuracy improved because they could finally *learn* — they could see the consequence of their timing and adjust in real time.

**Lesson learned:** In a game, clarity beats aesthetics. Always.

## "The Easy Songs Are Too Hard"

I'm a lifelong rhythm game player. My calibration for difficulty is wildly skewed. What I considered an "easy" beatmap would have a casual player's fingers cramping within 30 seconds.

The beatmap generator's difficulty scaling was tuned to my personal comfort zone, which turned out to be somewhere around "hard" for the general population. Easy songs had too many notes. The minimum note spacing was too tight. Patterns that felt natural to me were overwhelming to someone who'd never played a rhythm game.

We addressed this in several ways:

### Expanded Difficulty Settings

Instead of the original three difficulty levels (Easy, Normal, Hard), we expanded to five: **Chill, Easy, Normal, Hard, Expert.** Each level has dramatically different note density, pattern complexity, and speed settings.

**Chill mode** is nearly impossible to fail. Notes come slowly, patterns are simple, and the hit window is extremely forgiving. It's designed for people who want to enjoy the music and casually tap along without any stress. No score. No combo counter. Just vibes.

**Expert mode** is for the masochists — dense patterns, tight timing, and notes that require fast, precise inputs. This is where the leaderboard competition lives.

### Dynamic Difficulty Curves Within Songs

Even within a single difficulty level, the note density now ramps up gradually. The first 30 seconds of any song have reduced density while the player settles in. The system identifies the song's dynamic structure — verses, choruses, bridges — and scales difficulty accordingly. Quiet sections breathe. Intense sections push the player.

## "Why Can't I See What's Coming?"

Several players complained about **note approach visibility** — they couldn't see notes early enough to react. This was a direct consequence of the neumorphic design. Notes were soft, rounded, and blended naturally with the background. Gorgeous to look at. Terrible for gameplay readability at the default approach speed.

We made three changes:

1. **Increased the note approach distance** — notes now appear further from the hit zone, giving players more reaction time
2. **Added colored lane highlights** — as a note approaches, its lane subtly illuminates, providing an early warning
3. **Increased note contrast** — notes in gameplay mode now have slightly stronger shadows than other neumorphic elements, making them stand out from the background

The third change was the most impactful. By breaking the neumorphic consistency *just slightly* for game notes, we created a clear visual hierarchy: notes pop forward from the surface, drawing the eye.

## "The Menus Are Beautiful but Confusing"

Our neumorphic menus were visually striking, but several players struggled to identify what was clickable. Buttons, labels, and decorative panels all used the same visual treatment — raised neumorphic surfaces with soft shadows. Without clear affordances, players were clicking on static text expecting it to do something, and ignoring actual buttons because they looked like labels.

We added several clarifications:

- **Interactive elements** now have a subtle hover state that increases shadow intensity
- **Buttons** use the accent pink color for labels, differentiating them from plain text
- **Non-interactive surfaces** use slightly less pronounced shadows
- **Icons** accompany buttons where possible, providing additional context

Accessibility testing revealed that our focus states were also invisible. We added a visible focus ring for keyboard navigation — a subtle blue outline that appears when tabbing through the interface.

## "I Want to See My Progress"

Players wanted history. They wanted to see how they'd improved over time, which songs they'd mastered, where their weak points were. The original version had none of this — you finished a song, saw your score, and that was it. No record, no trends, no sense of progression.

We built a **player stats page** that tracks:

- **Total songs played** and time spent playing
- **Accuracy trend** — a graph showing how accuracy has changed over the last 30 days
- **Difficulty progression** — which difficulty levels the player has been conquering
- **Top scores** — personal bests across all songs
- **Most played songs** — the player's favorites by play count
- **Hit distribution** — a breakdown of Perfect/Great/Good/Miss ratios

This single feature transformed player retention. People came back not just to play new songs, but to watch their accuracy graph climb. The sense of measurable improvement is deeply motivating.

## "Can I Practice Just the Hard Part?"

This one caught me off guard. A player was struggling with a specific 10-second passage in a song and wanted to loop just that section to practice. We had no such feature — it was all or nothing.

We added a **practice mode** that lets players set loop points in a song. They can slow the audio down to 75% or 50% speed (with notes slowed to match), loop a difficult section, and gradually increase the speed as they improve. It's a feature that rhythm game veterans know and love, and its absence was glaring once someone pointed it out.

## "The Multiplayer Readying System Is Annoying"

In the original multiplayer flow, all players had to click "Ready" before a song started. If one person walked away for a drink, everyone else sat and waited. Frustrating.

We added a **ready timeout** — if all but one player are ready, a 30-second countdown begins. The unready player gets a notification. If they still don't ready up when the timer expires, the song starts anyway. Their score for that song will simply not be counted. Simple, but it eliminated a real source of friction in group play.

## The Ongoing Conversation

Feedback isn't a phase — it's a permanent state. We continue to collect player input through in-game surveys, community forums, Discord conversations, and playtesting sessions. Every major update goes through a feedback cycle.

The most important lesson from all of this: **you are not your player.** Your instincts are calibrated to your own experience and skill level. The only way to build a game that works for real humans is to put it in front of real humans, listen to what they say, and have the humility to change what isn't working — even if you thought it was perfect.

Slice It! is a better game because of the people who played it and told me the truth. I'm deeply grateful for every piece of feedback, especially the harsh ones.

title: The Future of Slice It!: What's Coming Next
description: A look at the roadmap for Slice It! — new modes, competitive features, and our long-term vision.
content:
## Looking Ahead

Ten months ago, Slice It! was a prototype — a single-player rhythm game with rough beatmap generation and a half-finished UI. Today, it's a real platform with multiplayer, a community song library, thousands of plays, and an identity that's uniquely its own.

But we're far from finished. If anything, the foundation we've built has only expanded the canvas of what's possible. I want to share where Slice It! is heading — the features we're actively developing, the ideas we're prototyping, and the long-term vision that keeps us motivated.

This isn't a polished marketing roadmap. This is an honest look at what's on the workbench. Some of these features will ship exactly as described. Some will evolve through playtesting. Some might be cut entirely if they don't work in practice. That's game development.

## Ranked Competitive Mode

The most requested feature since launch. Players want a structured competitive system with visible stakes — not just leaderboards, but a **ranking system** that reflects skill over time.

Here's what we're designing:

### Seasonal Ranks

Players will be placed into ranked tiers based on their competitive performance across a season (roughly 3 months). We're considering a tiered system: **Bronze, Silver, Gold, Platinum, Diamond, and Maestro.** Each tier has subdivisions, and players advance or drop based on match results.

### Ranked Match Format

A ranked match will work as a best-of-three format between two players. Songs are selected through a **pick-ban system** — both players see a pool of 5 songs, each bans one, and they alternate picks for the remaining three. This adds strategic depth beyond raw timing skill. Knowing your opponent's weaknesses, choosing songs that favor your strengths, and banning songs you struggle with all become part of the game.

### Anti-Smurf Measures

New accounts will go through **placement matches** (10 games) before receiving their initial rank. We'll also monitor for statistically improbable performance patterns that suggest a skilled player deliberately deranking.

## Custom Beatmap Editor

The automatic beatmap generator is Slice It!'s signature feature, and it's gotten remarkably good. But it's not perfect — and for some songs, a human mapper can create a more satisfying experience.

We're building a **browser-based beatmap editor** that lets advanced users create and share hand-crafted maps. The editor will feature:

- **Timeline view** with waveform visualization and a scrollable note placement interface
- **Snap-to-beat guides** that help mappers place notes on rhythmically consistent positions
- **Live preview** — play through your map in real time while editing, adjusting notes on the fly
- **Import from auto-gen** — start with the automatically generated beatmap and refine it, rather than starting from scratch
- **Community sharing** — hand-crafted maps will appear alongside auto-generated ones in the song library, tagged as "community maps"

The auto-gen maps aren't going anywhere. The editor is an addition, not a replacement. The philosophy remains: any song should be instantly playable without waiting for someone to manually chart it. But for players who want to craft the definitive map for their favorite song, the tools should exist.

## New Game Modes

The standard "notes scroll toward a hit zone" format is the core of Slice It!, but we're experimenting with alternative modes that offer different experiences:

### Zen Mode

No score. No misses. No failure state. Notes flow and you tap along. Any input registers as a hit. This is for pure relaxation — listening to music and engaging with it physically without any pressure. Background visuals respond to your inputs, creating a personalized light show synchronized to the music.

### Endurance Mode

A continuous stream of songs with no breaks. Your health bar depletes on misses and regenerates on hits. How long can you survive? The difficulty gradually ramps up over time, starting at Chill and escalating toward Expert. Endurance runs are tracked on a separate leaderboard ranked by total time survived.

### Speed Mode

The normal beatmap, but the scroll speed increases over the course of the song. By the final chorus, notes are flying at double the normal speed. It tests a completely different skill set — not just timing, but rapid visual processing and adaptability.

### Tag Team Mode

A cooperative multiplayer mode where two players share the same note highway. Notes are color-coded — blue notes are Player 1's responsibility, pink notes are Player 2's. Miss a note, and your partner's screen flashes as a warning. Your combined score is what counts. It's rhythm gaming as a trust exercise.

## Improved Audio Analysis

The beatmap generator is our core technology, and it's under continuous improvement. Key areas of active research:

### Genre-Aware Generation

Different genres have different rhythmic conventions. An EDM track and a jazz piece have fundamentally different structures, and the beatmap generator should understand that. We're training genre classification models that detect the style of a song and adjust generation parameters accordingly — onset sensitivity, note density curves, pattern preferences.

### Vocal Isolation

Using spectral processing techniques inspired by tools like LALAL.ai, we're experimenting with isolating vocal lines from the mix and using them to generate dedicated note lanes. Imagine a lane that follows the melody while other lanes track the percussion and bass. This creates maps that feel like you're playing *every part* of the song.

### BPM and Downbeat Detection

While we intentionally don't rely on BPM metadata, knowing the song's tempo and where the downbeats fall improves map quality significantly. We're implementing a BPM detection algorithm that analyzes the autocorrelation of the onset detection signal to find the most likely tempo and phase. This information helps the post-processor align notes to rhythmically sensible positions.

## Social Features

Music is social, and Slice It! should be too. Beyond the existing multiplayer system and community library, we're planning:

### Player Profiles

Public profiles showing a player's stats, favorite songs, recent activity, and competitive rank. Other players can follow profiles to see when friends are online or when they set a new high score.

### Clubs

Groups of players who share songs, compete in club leaderboards, and organize multiplayer sessions. Think of them as Discord servers within Slice It! — each with their own song playlists, chat, and weekly challenges.

### Replay Sharing

After finishing a song, players can save and share a replay. The replay file stores input data and the beatmap, allowing anyone to watch exactly how a top score was achieved. This serves both a social function (showing off) and an educational one (learning from better players).

## Mobile Support

This is a big one and one of our most ambitious goals. Slice It! currently works on desktop browsers with keyboard input. But rhythm games are fantastic on mobile — touch input is natural and satisfying.

The challenges are significant:

- **Touch input mapping** — redesigning the note highway for taps and swipes rather than key presses
- **Performance** — maintaining smooth rendering on mobile GPUs and browsers
- **Audio latency** — mobile browsers have notoriously high audio latency, making calibration even more critical
- **Responsive design** — the neumorphic UI needs to work beautifully on small screens

We're prototyping a mobile-optimized game mode with a simplified layout — four lanes across the bottom of the screen that you tap directly. Early tests are promising.

## The Long-Term Vision

When I step back and think about where I want Slice It! to be in two years, the vision is clear:

**Slice It! should be the place where music and play converge.** A platform where any song is instantly playable, where players compete and connect in meaningful ways, where a thriving community of music lovers shares discoveries and celebrates great gameplay.

Not a niche rhythm game for hardcore players. Not a casual toy that people try once and forget. A living, growing ecosystem that sits at the intersection of music, competition, and community.

The technical foundation is solid. The community is growing. The design identity is distinct and beloved. Now it's about execution — shipping features, listening to feedback, iterating relentlessly, and never losing sight of the thing that makes Slice It! special: the feeling of pressing a key at exactly the right moment, hearing the note ring out, and knowing *you* are part of the music.

Thank you for following along on this journey. Whether you've been here since the first devlog or you're just discovering Slice It! now, I'm grateful for your time and your attention. The best is still ahead.

See you on the leaderboard.

title: Introducing: Slice It!
description: A new rhythm game prototype built with Next.js and the Web Audio API.
content:
We're excited to unveil our latest prototype: **Slice It!**

It's a high-energy rhythm game that runs directly in your browser. No downloads, no installation—just pure, uncut beats.

## The Concept

We wanted to build something that feels responsive and "juicy" without relying on heavy game engines like Unity or Unreal. Using the **Web Audio API** for precise timing and HTML5 Canvas for rendering, we've achieved a smooth 60fps experience on most devices.

### Core Features at Launch:
- **Precise Timing**: Audio scheduling is handled on a separate thread logic to ensure notes hit exactly when they should.
- **Dynamic Visuals**: Neon aesthetics that react to the music.
- **Combo System**: Rack up points and keep the multiplier going!

Give it a try and let us know what you think. We have big plans for future updates, including custom track uploads and more complex note types.

title: Devlog: Ramping Up the Difficulty
description: Adding an Osu-style health system and multi-lane gameplay to Slice It.
content:
The feedback on **Slice It** has been amazing, but you asked for more challenge. We heard you.

Today's update introduces two major mechanics that fundamentally change how the game is played.

## The Health System

We've moved away from a simple "fail on miss" approach to a more forgiving (but stressful) health bar system, similar to **Osu!**.
- **Miss a note**: Lose 15% health.
- **Hit a note**: Recover a small amount.
- **Reach 0%**: System Failure.

This allows players to recover from a bad slip-up if they can regain their rhythm quickly.

## Stereo Lanes

Why stick to one lane when you have two ears? We've split the track into **Top** and **Bottom** lanes.
- **'F' Key**: Controls the Top Lane.
- **'J' Key**: Controls the Bottom Lane.

This adds a layer of coordination complexity that feels incredibly satisfying once you lock in.

title: Adding WebSocket Multiplayer to a Rhythm Game
description: The technical journey of adding real-time WebSocket multiplayer to Slice It! with lobby support.

title: Performance Optimization for Real-Time Audio Games
description: How we keep Slice It! running at 60fps while processing real-time audio and rendering on Canvas.

title: Building Slice It!'s Rhythm Engine From Scratch
description: How we built a precise rhythm game engine in the browser — from audio sync to community beatmaps.
content:
## The Beat Must Be Exact

Rhythm games are unforgiving. A platformer can have 50 milliseconds of input lag and still feel responsive. A rhythm game with 50 milliseconds of desync is broken. Players don't consciously measure latency — they **feel** it. When the visual cue says "now" and the audio says "not yet," the entire experience collapses.

Building Slice It! — our browser-based rhythm game — meant solving this precision problem in the most challenging runtime environment possible: a web browser. This is the story of how we built the rhythm engine, what Beat Saber taught us about the genre, and how community beatmaps turned a single-player game into a living platform.

## The Beat Saber Effect

**Beat Saber** didn't invent the rhythm game, but it redefined the genre for a generation. Released in 2018 by Beat Games (later acquired by Meta), it proved that rhythm gameplay could be visceral, physical, and endlessly replayable. By 2024, Beat Saber had sold over 6 million copies and become one of VR's defining titles.

Beat Saber's genius is the mapping between music and motion. Notes don't just arrive on beat — they arrive in **spatial patterns** that turn your body into an instrument. The best Beat Saber maps feel choreographed, like dancing with a lightsaber.

Slice It! takes direct inspiration from this philosophy. We can't replicate the physicality of VR in a browser, but we can replicate the core design principle: **every input should feel musical**. When you tap, swipe, or hold in Slice It!, the action should feel like it belongs in the song. Not just on beat, but on the right beat, with the right weight, creating the right emphasis.

This means our rhythm engine can't just track timing — it has to understand musical structure.

## The Web Audio API: Power and Pain

The foundation of our rhythm engine is the **Web Audio API**, the browser's built-in audio processing system. The Web Audio API is remarkably powerful — it provides sample-accurate timing, real-time audio processing, and a node-based architecture that can handle complex audio routing.

But it also has quirks that make rhythm game development challenging:

### The Autoplay Problem

Modern browsers block audio playback until the user interacts with the page. This is a sensible policy for preventing obnoxious auto-playing ads, but it means a rhythm game can't start audio on its own. We solve this with a deliberate "Start" interaction — when the player taps to begin a song, we initialize the AudioContext and begin playback in the same user gesture handler.

### The Clock Problem

JavaScript's `Date.now()` and `performance.now()` provide millisecond-resolution timing, but they're not synchronized with audio playback. The Web Audio API has its own clock — `AudioContext.currentTime` — which runs on the audio thread and provides high-resolution, drift-free timing.

Our rhythm engine uses `AudioContext.currentTime` as the **single source of truth** for all timing decisions. Visual elements, input windows, and scoring all reference this clock. This eliminates the class of bugs where visual and audio timing drift apart over the course of a song.

### The Latency Problem

Audio output latency varies dramatically across devices and browsers. A desktop running Chrome might have 10ms of output latency. A mobile device running Safari might have 100ms. This latency is the delay between when the browser sends audio data to the sound card and when the player actually hears it.

We handle this with a **calibration system**. Before their first session, players tap along to a metronome, and we measure the average offset between their taps and the audio beats. This offset is stored and applied to all timing calculations, effectively personalizing the engine to each player's device.

## The Beatmap Format

A beatmap is the heart of a rhythm game level. It defines when notes appear, what type they are, and where they're positioned. Our beatmap format is a JSON structure with the following key components:

- **Song metadata** — title, artist, BPM, duration, difficulty rating
- **Timing points** — BPM changes, time signature changes, and offset adjustments
- **Note events** — timestamp, type (tap, hold, swipe, multi-tap), position, and duration
- **Effect events** — visual effects, camera movements, and background changes synchronized to the music

Each note event is defined relative to the song's beat structure, not absolute time. A note at beat 4.5 of measure 12 stays at beat 4.5 of measure 12 regardless of BPM. This makes beatmaps resilient to timing adjustments and allows us to support songs with tempo changes.

### Scoring and Judgment

When a player inputs at a given moment, the engine compares the input timestamp against the nearest expected note. The timing window is divided into judgment tiers:

- **Perfect** — within 25ms of the target
- **Great** — within 50ms
- **Good** — within 100ms
- **Miss** — beyond 100ms or no input

These windows are tighter than most casual rhythm games but looser than hardcore titles like Sound Voltex or IIDX. We tuned them through extensive playtesting to find the sweet spot where skilled players feel rewarded but casual players aren't constantly failing.

Scoring uses a weighted system where judgment quality, combo length, and difficulty multipliers all contribute. The combo system — where consecutive non-miss hits build a multiplier — creates the risk-reward tension that makes rhythm games compelling. Breaking a 200-hit combo on a Perfect run is devastating, and that emotional weight is what keeps players coming back.

## Custom Beatmap Generation

Creating beatmaps by hand is an art form. The Beat Saber modding community has produced thousands of custom maps, and the best mappers are celebrated artists in their own right. But hand-crafted maps don't scale — especially for a platform that wants to support community song uploads.

We built a **procedural beatmap generator** that analyzes uploaded audio and produces playable maps. The algorithm works in stages:

1. **Onset detection** — identify when musical events (beats, notes, percussive hits) occur in the audio
2. **Beat tracking** — determine the song's tempo and meter
3. **Energy analysis** — measure the intensity of the audio over time to create difficulty curves
4. **Pattern generation** — place notes based on detected onsets, filtered by difficulty level and constrained by ergonomic rules (no physically impossible patterns)
5. **Flow optimization** — adjust note placement to create smooth movement patterns that feel natural to play

The generator produces maps at three difficulty levels. Easy maps follow the main beat and melody. Medium maps add syncopation and faster patterns. Hard maps track detailed rhythmic subdivisions and demand precise, rapid inputs.

Generated maps aren't as good as hand-crafted ones — they lack the intentional choreographic quality of a skilled mapper's work. But they're surprisingly playable, and they allow any song in our library to be immediately available at multiple difficulty levels.

## Community Song Uploads

The feature that transformed Slice It! from a rhythm game into a rhythm **platform** is community song uploads. Players can upload their own audio, and our system processes it through the beatmap generator to create playable levels.

The upload pipeline works as follows:

- **Audio upload** — player uploads an MP3 or WAV file
- **Audio processing** — server-side analysis extracts timing data and generates beatmaps
- **Review queue** — generated maps go through a brief automated quality check (minimum note density, playability verification)
- **Publication** — approved maps appear in the community library with the uploader's credit

We also support **community mapping** — players can use our browser-based beatmap editor to create hand-crafted maps for any song. These maps are tagged separately from generated ones, and the best community maps are featured in our curated playlists.

### The Copyright Question

We want to be transparent about copyright. Community song uploads operate under the same legal framework as user-generated content on any platform. We implement content identification and respond to takedown requests in compliance with the DMCA. Players are responsible for uploading content they have the right to share, and our terms of service reflect this.

## Visual Sync

Audio-visual synchronization in a rhythm game isn't just about timing — it's about **anticipation**. Notes need to appear before the player needs to hit them, giving visual warning that maps to the audio cue. The travel time of a note from its spawn point to the hit zone must be consistent and predictable.

We render note tracks using a custom canvas-based renderer that maintains a steady visual frame rate independent of the audio clock. The renderer interpolates note positions between audio timing events, creating smooth visual motion even when the display refresh rate doesn't perfectly align with the audio sample rate.

Visual effects — particle bursts on perfect hits, screen shakes on misses, combo fire on long streaks — are purely cosmetic and rendered independently of the timing system. They never interfere with note visibility or input detection. Eye candy is important, but **clarity is sacred**.

## Lessons From the Rhythm Trenches

Building a rhythm engine in the browser taught us several lessons:

- **Trust the audio clock.** Every timing bug we encountered was caused by using a non-audio clock for timing decisions. `AudioContext.currentTime` is the only truth.
- **Calibration is not optional.** Device latency varies too much to use a fixed offset. Per-device calibration is the difference between "this game feels off" and "this game feels perfect."
- **Generated maps need constraints.** An algorithm that places notes on every detected onset creates unplayable chaos. Ergonomic constraints — maximum note density, minimum spacing, pattern variety — are essential.
- **Community content changes everything.** The moment players could upload their own songs, engagement transformed. People play their music differently than they play curated selections.

Slice It! started as an experiment: can we build a rhythm game that feels precise in a browser? The answer is yes — with careful engineering, creative compromise, and respect for the genre's unforgiving standards. The Web Audio API gives us the tools. The community gives us the music. The browser gives us the reach.

Every beat matters. Every frame matters. And every player who hits a Perfect streak on their favorite song makes the engineering worth it.

title: Slice It: Redesigning the Rhythm Engine UI
description: A detailed look at the UI redesign process for Slice It: Redesigning the Rhythm Engine UI.
content:
UI design is never finished. It evolves. For Slice It: Redesigning the Rhythm Engine UI, our goal was to bridge the gap between functionality and aesthetic brilliance. In this post, we'll dive deep into the specific challenges we faced and the solutions we implemented.

## The Objective

The primary goal of the Slice It: Redesigning the Rhythm Engine UI redesign was to enhance user engagement while maintaining technical efficiency. We wanted users to feel a sense of fluid interaction, where every click or hover provided meaningful feedback. This required a fundamental rethink of our existing components and spiritual alignment with the overall rmhstudios brand.

We started by auditing our current implementation. What was working? What felt clunky? What was redundant? By stripping back the unnecessary elements, we were able to focus on the core user journey. For Slice It: Redesigning the Rhythm Engine UI, this meant focusing on clarity and speed.

## Iterative Design Process

Design at rmhstudios is iterative. We don't just build once and ship. We prototype, test, and refine. For this particular update, we went through three major stages of development:

1.  **Conceptual Wireframing**: Mapping out the raw data flow and user interaction points. We ignored visuals at this stage to focus purely on UX.
2.  **Visual Prototyping**: Applying our signature neon aesthetic. This involved heavy use of CSS variables to ensure consistency across light and dark modes.
3.  **Technical Integration**: Merging the design with our Next.js and Tailwind architecture. Performance was a key metric here—we couldn't let visual polish compromise load times.

One specific challenge was handling responsive layout shifts. As screens get smaller, information density needs to be managed carefully. We implemented custom media queries and flexible grid systems to ensure that Slice It: Redesigning the Rhythm Engine UI looks stunning on everything from a widescreen monitor to a handheld device.

## Key Technical Details

From a technical perspective, we relied heavily on modern CSS features. We used `clamp()` for fluid typography and `grid-template-areas` for complex layouts. This allowed us to write cleaner code that is easier to maintain. We also leaned into the power of Framer Motion for micro-animations. These subtle movements—like a button gently pulsing or a card sliding into view—add a layer of polish that makes the web feel alive.

We also prioritized accessibility. Every redesign is an opportunity to improve. We audited our color contrast ratios and ensured that all interactive elements are keyboard-navigable. A beautiful UI is useless if it's not inclusive.

## The Impact

Since implementing these changes, we've seen a noticeable improvement in user retention and satisfaction. The feedback has been overwhelmingly positive. Users appreciate the cleaner aesthetic and the more intuitive navigation. It's a testament to the idea that small, thoughtful changes can have a massive impact on the overall experience.

We are particularly proud of the performance gains. By optimizing our asset delivery and refining our component logic, we've managed to reduce the cumulative layout shift (CLS) and improve the largest contentful paint (LCP) across the site.

## Looking Forward

This is just the beginning. As we move closer to the launch of Project Vega and further updates to our existing games, we'll continue to push the boundaries of what's possible in web-based game UI. We're already experimenting with more advanced shader effects and interactive 3D elements that sit directly in the DOM.

The journey of design is continuous. We're always learning, always growing, and always looking for ways to make rmhstudios.com the best it can be. Stay tuned for more updates as we continue to refine our vision.

## Conclusion

The redesign of Slice It: Redesigning the Rhythm Engine UI represents a significant milestone in our design evolution. It's not just about making things look "pretty"; it's about creating an experience that is functional, accessible, and delightful. We hope this deep dive into our process provides some insight into how we approach UI at rmhstudios.

What are your thoughts on this new direction? Do you have suggestions for future redesigns? We'd love to hear from you in the community Discord or via our feedback forms. Your input helps shape the future of this studio.

Thanks for reading, and keep on creating!

title: Neumorphic Design in a Rhythm Game
description: Why we chose neumorphism for Slice It! and how its soft aesthetic sets it apart.
content:
## Against the Grain

Open any rhythm game and you'll see the same visual language: dark backgrounds, neon highlights, aggressive particle effects, high-contrast color schemes that scream "GAMER." It's a well-established aesthetic, and for good reason — it's energetic, it's exciting, and it pairs well with music.

So naturally, I decided to do the exact opposite.

Slice It! uses a **neumorphic design system** built on soft shadows, rounded corners, muted blues and pinks, and a predominantly light color palette. It looks nothing like a typical rhythm game. And that was entirely the point.

## What Is Neumorphism?

Neumorphism (a blend of "new" and "skeuomorphism") is a design style that creates the illusion of elements extruding from or pressing into a surface. Instead of flat UI elements sitting on a background, neumorphic elements appear to be part of the background — raised slightly or recessed slightly, with soft light and shadow creating the depth effect.

The key ingredients are:

- **A uniform background color** (typically a soft gray, cream, or pastel)
- **Two shadows per element** — a lighter shadow on one side (simulating light hitting a raised edge) and a darker shadow on the opposite side (simulating the element casting a shadow)
- **Rounded corners** everywhere
- **Minimal use of borders** — depth is conveyed through shadow, not outlines
- **Soft, muted color palette** — no harsh contrasts

Here's how a typical neumorphic button looks in CSS:

```css
.neu-button {
  background: #e8edf5;
  border: none;
  border-radius: 16px;
  padding: 16px 32px;
  color: #5a6e8a;
  font-weight: 600;
  box-shadow:
    6px 6px 12px rgba(163, 177, 198, 0.6),
    -6px -6px 12px rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.8);
  transition: box-shadow 0.2s ease;
}

.neu-button:active {
  box-shadow:
    inset 4px 4px 8px rgba(163, 177, 198, 0.6),
    inset -4px -4px 8px rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.8);
}
```

When you press the button, the shadow shifts from outer to inner, creating the sensation that the element is being pressed *into* the surface. It's satisfying in a way that's hard to describe until you feel it.

## Why Neumorphism for Slice It!

The decision to use neumorphism came from a few converging motivations:

### 1. Differentiation

The rhythm game market is visually homogeneous. Neon-on-dark is the default. By going with a soft, light design, Slice It! immediately stands out. Players who see screenshots or videos think, "That doesn't look like any rhythm game I've seen before." That initial curiosity is invaluable for discoverability.

### 2. Emotional Tone

I wanted Slice It! to feel **approachable**. The neon aesthetic is exciting, but it can also be intimidating — it signals hardcore, competitive, sweaty-palms gaming. Neumorphism signals calm, polish, and warmth. It says, "This is a chill space to enjoy music." That doesn't mean the gameplay isn't challenging — it absolutely is — but the visual environment doesn't add stress to the challenge.

### 3. Focus on Content

In a neumorphic UI, the content is the star. The soft, uniform background recedes, letting song names, artwork, scores, and gameplay elements take center stage. There's no visual noise competing for attention. Everything you need to see is clear and prominent without needing to shout.

### 4. Contrast with RMH Studios

The main RMH Studios website uses a dark theme with neon accents — glowing borders, vibrant gradients, cyberpunk-inspired visuals. Slice It! deliberately breaks from that aesthetic. Each project at RMH Studios has its own visual identity, and Slice It!'s soft palette makes it instantly recognizable as its own thing while still feeling related to the broader portfolio.

## The Color Palette

The Slice It! palette centers on three families:

- **Soft blues** (`#c5d5e8`, `#8aacd4`, `#5a8abf`) — primary interactive elements, selected states, progress indicators
- **Soft pinks** (`#f0d4de`, `#e8a8c0`, `#d4799a`) — accent elements, highlights, important actions
- **Whites and light grays** (`#f2f5f9`, `#e8edf5`, `#dde4ef`) — backgrounds, surfaces, card bodies

These colors are deliberately desaturated. Nothing is vivid. Everything feels like it's been gently washed — as if you're looking at the UI through a soft-focus filter. The effect is calming and cohesive.

For text, we use a muted navy (`#3a4a5e`) instead of true black. This reduces harsh contrast against the light background and contributes to the overall softness.

## Neumorphism in Gameplay

The neumorphic style extends beyond menus into the **gameplay canvas** itself. Notes approaching the hit zone have soft shadows and rounded shapes. The hit zone is a recessed neumorphic element at the bottom of the screen — notes seem to sink into it when you hit them.

Hit feedback uses the same visual language. A "Perfect" hit produces a gentle radial glow in soft blue. A "Good" hit produces a softer, smaller glow. Misses produce a brief, subtle inward shadow — the surface dips where the note should have been caught.

Even the score display, combo counter, and accuracy meter are neumorphic elements floating above the game canvas. They feel like physical instruments embedded in a dashboard rather than flat overlays.

```css
.score-display {
  background: #e8edf5;
  border-radius: 20px;
  padding: 12px 24px;
  box-shadow:
    4px 4px 8px rgba(163, 177, 198, 0.5),
    -4px -4px 8px rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.7);
  font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums;
}
```

## Challenges and Trade-offs

Neumorphism isn't without its problems. I want to be honest about the trade-offs.

### Accessibility Concerns

The soft shadows and low-contrast color relationships that make neumorphism beautiful can also make it harder to perceive for some users. We address this with a **high-contrast mode** that adds subtle borders to interactive elements and increases the shadow intensity. It breaks the pure neumorphic aesthetic slightly, but inclusivity matters more than visual purity.

### Readability at Small Sizes

Neumorphic elements need a minimum size to read correctly. At small sizes, the shadows compress and the element loses its dimensional quality, becoming an indistinct blob. We enforced minimum sizing guidelines across the design system to prevent this.

### Dark Mode Expectations

Many players expect a dark mode. Neumorphism works in dark themes, but it requires a complete reskin — you can't just invert colors. Dark neumorphism uses lighter shadow on the top-left and deeper shadow on the bottom-right against a dark gray base. We plan to add a dark neumorphic theme eventually, but it's a significant design effort, not a simple toggle.

### Performance on Low-End Devices

Box shadows are more GPU-intensive than flat elements. On low-end devices, we reduce shadow complexity and disable transition animations. The neumorphic identity is preserved through rounded corners and color palette alone, even when shadows are simplified.

## The Evolution

The current design is far from where we started. Early iterations were *too* subtle — buttons didn't look clickable, active states were hard to distinguish from resting states, and the overall UI felt like a static image rather than an interactive application.

Over months of iteration, we found the right balance. Interactive elements have slightly more pronounced shadows than decorative ones. Active and hover states produce noticeable depth changes. The pink accent color draws attention to primary actions without being garish.

Design is never finished. But the neumorphic foundation of Slice It! gives us a visual identity that's unique, cohesive, and instantly recognizable. In a sea of neon rhythm games, we're the soft one — and that's exactly where we want to be.

title: Why Every Game Needs a Social Layer
description: Games are social spaces first and entertainment products second — and we build accordingly.
content:
## The Loneliness Problem

In May 2023, U.S. Surgeon General Dr. Vivek Murthy issued an advisory titled *Our Epidemic of Loneliness and Isolation*, calling social disconnection a public health crisis on par with smoking and obesity. The advisory cited research showing that lacking social connection increases the risk of premature death by 26% and is associated with higher rates of depression, anxiety, and dementia.

This isn't abstract public health data. It describes the lived experience of millions of people — particularly young adults — who spend hours each day in digital environments but still feel fundamentally alone.

Games can be part of the solution. Not as therapy, not as a replacement for real relationships, but as **spaces where genuine human connection happens naturally**, as a byproduct of shared experience. This is why we believe every game needs a social layer — not because social features drive engagement metrics, but because connection is what makes games matter.

## Games as Social Infrastructure

The most culturally significant gaming moments of recent years haven't been about gameplay — they've been about togetherness.

**Fortnite's in-game concerts** — Travis Scott's Astronomical event in April 2020 drew 12.3 million concurrent players. Ariana Grande's Rift Tour in August 2021 similarly attracted millions. These weren't just spectacles; they were shared experiences that people remember and talk about years later.

**Roblox as a hangout space.** Roblox's 71.5 million daily active users (as of Q3 2024) don't just play games — they socialize. For millions of kids and teenagers, Roblox is where they meet friends after school, the way previous generations met at malls or playgrounds.

**Animal Crossing during lockdown.** When COVID-19 lockdowns began in 2020, Animal Crossing: New Horizons became a lifeline. People held weddings, graduation ceremonies, and birthday parties on their islands. The game's value wasn't its mechanics — it was the social space it provided.

**Among Us as social glue.** Among Us exploded in 2020 not because of its game design (which had existed since 2018 with minimal traction) but because it gave friend groups a reason to get on voice chat and laugh together.

The pattern is clear: **the games that matter most are the ones that create shared social context.** The gameplay is the scaffold. The connection is the building.

## How We Build Social Into Everything

At RMH Studios, social features aren't an afterthought bolted onto finished games. They're part of the foundational architecture.

### RMHbox: Social by Nature

RMHbox is a party game platform, so it's inherently social — but the design decisions that make it *effectively* social are intentional:

**Lobbies are gathering spaces.** When you create or join an RMHbox room, you're not immediately thrown into a game. You're in a lobby where you can chat, see who's present, and build anticipation. The lobby is the porch before the party.

**Games are designed for reactions.** Our minigames are tuned for moments that provoke laughter, surprise, and friendly trash talk. A well-designed party game creates stories that players retell — "Remember when you fell off the platform three times in a row?" These micro-stories are the raw material of friendship.

**Spectating is a feature.** Players waiting between rounds or arriving mid-game can watch the action. Spectating transforms waiting from dead time into entertainment, and it lets newcomers observe before participating — lowering the social anxiety of joining a group.

### RMHTube: Watching Together

RMHTube — our watch party platform — is built entirely on the premise that watching alone is worse than watching together. Synchronized video playback, real-time chat, and shared playlists turn passive media consumption into active social experience.

The technical challenge is interesting: keeping video playback synchronized across multiple clients with different network conditions and buffer states requires constant correction. But the social outcome is worth the engineering complexity. People use RMHTube for movie nights, music video sessions, and watching gaming tournaments together. The content is the excuse. The company is the point.

### RMH Study: Productive Together

RMH Study applies the same social logic to productivity. Pomodoro rooms let people work alongside each other in timed focus sessions, with break periods for casual conversation.

This mirrors the real-world phenomenon of "body doubling" — the productivity boost people get from simply being in the presence of others who are also working. Coworking spaces, library study halls, and coffee shop work sessions all leverage this effect. RMH Study digitizes it.

The ambient social presence — knowing someone else is in the room, focused on their own work — creates gentle accountability without pressure. It's social without being distracting.

### RMH Type: Competing Together

Typing tests are inherently solo activities. RMH Type makes them social by adding real-time multiplayer races. You're not just measuring your WPM against a historical average — you're racing against someone right now, watching their cursor advance alongside yours.

Competition is a social act. It requires an opponent, which means it requires another person. Even the simplest competitive mechanic — "who can type this passage faster?" — transforms a solitary exercise into a shared experience with stakes, tension, and resolution.

### Daily Games: Rituals Together

RMHdle and RMHConnections, our Discord daily games, create social touchpoints that recur every single day. When everyone in a community solves the same puzzle, the puzzle becomes a conversation starter. "How did you get today's RMHdle?" is a greeting, a bonding ritual, and a low-stakes competition wrapped in five words.

## The Design Principles

Across all our products, a few social design principles guide our decisions:

### Lower the Barrier to Social Interaction

Every layer of friction between a player and a social experience is a layer where they might give up. No account required for first play. No friend codes to exchange. No complex room setup. Send a link, click a link, you're in a room with people.

### Make Being Together the Default

Solo modes exist where they make sense (Signal Forge is a single-player game), but wherever possible, the default experience includes other people. The multiplayer lobby is the home screen, not a menu option buried three clicks deep.

### Support Asynchronous Sociality

Not all social experiences need to be real-time. Daily games let players participate on their own schedule while still being part of a shared experience. Leaderboards let players compete without being online simultaneously. Shared profiles let players express identity persistently.

### Create Stories, Not Just Scores

The most social games are the ones that generate memorable moments — unexpected outcomes, dramatic reversals, absurd situations. We design for stories. A minigame where someone wins by one point after a comeback is more social than one where the best player wins every time, because the comeback creates a story worth telling.

### Respect Boundaries

Social features should invite, not coerce. We don't send push notifications saying "your friends are playing without you." We don't create FOMO mechanics that punish players for not being constantly available. Social features work best when they make connection easy and absence painless.

## The Broader Responsibility

We don't think games alone can solve the loneliness epidemic. That's a societal challenge that requires systemic solutions — urban design, healthcare, education, labor policy. Games are one small piece.

But games are a piece that reaches billions of people, many of them in the demographics most affected by social isolation. If we can make our games places where people feel genuinely connected — even briefly, even casually — that's a meaningful contribution.

The Surgeon General's advisory noted that digital tools can either exacerbate or alleviate isolation, depending on how they're designed. Passive social media consumption tends to increase loneliness. Active, reciprocal social experiences tend to decrease it. Games — when designed for genuine interaction rather than solitary engagement loops — fall into the second category.

## Social Is Not a Feature

Here's the core conviction: **social is not a feature you add to a game. It's the reason games exist.**

Humans have been playing games together for thousands of years — not because the games were sophisticated, but because playing together is one of the most natural forms of human connection. A ball, a set of rules, and some friends. That's the oldest social technology on Earth.

Every game we build at RMH Studios starts with a question: "How will this bring people together?" If we don't have a compelling answer, we rethink the design.

Because a game you play alone is entertainment. A game you play with others is a relationship. And relationships are what we're really building.

title: Sound Design Philosophy for Browser Games
description: Good game audio is invisible when it works and devastating when it's missing. Here's how we approach sound on the web.
content:
## The Invisible Art

In his seminal 2008 book **"Game Feel: A Game Designer's Guide to Virtual Sensation,"** Steve Swink argues that the feeling of interacting with a game — what he calls "game feel" — is built on three pillars: real-time control, simulated space, and **polish**. Audio sits at the heart of that third pillar.

A button click without a sound feels broken. A jump without a whoosh feels weightless. An explosion without a boom feels fake. Players don't consciously think about these sounds — they process them instinctively, and the absence registers as wrongness before the mind can articulate why.

We think about audio constantly at RMH Studios. We build browser games, which means we build on the **Web Audio API**, which means we operate in a unique and sometimes frustrating sonic landscape. This post is about our philosophy, our tools, and the hard-won lessons we've learned about making games that sound as good as they play.

## The Web Audio API: Power and Constraints

The **Web Audio API** is one of the web platform's most underappreciated capabilities. Introduced in Chrome in 2011, standardized by the W3C, and now supported across all major browsers, it provides a node-based audio processing graph that can handle:

- Sample playback with precise timing
- Real-time audio synthesis (oscillators, noise generators)
- Spatial audio (panning, distance attenuation)
- Audio effects (reverb, delay, compression, filtering)
- Real-time analysis (FFT, waveform visualization)
- Custom audio processing via AudioWorklets

For a browser API, this is remarkably powerful. Professional audio middleware like **FMOD** and **Wwise** — the industry standard tools used in AAA game development — offer more features (adaptive music systems, complex event hierarchies, integrated authoring tools), but the Web Audio API provides a solid foundation for building similar systems from scratch.

Where the Web Audio API falls short compared to FMOD/Wwise:

- **No built-in authoring tool.** FMOD and Wwise provide visual editors where sound designers can build complex audio events without writing code. The Web Audio API is pure code.
- **No middleware-level abstraction.** In FMOD, you can define an event like "footstep_on_grass" that automatically selects from a pool of variations, adjusts pitch randomly, and attenuates based on distance. In the Web Audio API, you build all of that logic yourself.
- **Limited codec support.** Browsers support MP3, AAC, OGG Vorbis, WAV, and Opus, but not the proprietary compressed formats that middleware uses for efficient streaming.

Despite these limitations, we've built audio systems for our games that handle dynamic music, responsive sound effects, spatial audio, and real-time synthesis — all within the Web Audio API.

## The Autoplay Problem

If you've built anything with audio on the web in the last several years, you've hit this wall.

In 2018, **Chrome implemented an autoplay policy** that blocks AudioContext creation until a user interacts with the page (a click, tap, or keypress). Other browsers followed with similar policies. The intent was good — nobody likes a website that blasts audio unprompted — but for game developers, it created a design challenge.

Our solution is straightforward but required thought:

- **We resume the AudioContext on the first user interaction**, typically the "Play" or "Start" button that every game naturally has.
- **We design our games so that no critical audio plays before user interaction.** Title screens use visual cues; the audio experience begins when the gameplay begins.
- **We provide clear volume controls and mute options** in every game, because respecting the player's audio preferences is as important as having good audio in the first place.

## Audio Design Across the RMH Catalog

Different games demand different audio approaches. Here's how we think about sound across our catalog:

### RMHbox (Party Games)

Audio in party games serves two purposes: **feedback and energy**. Every action a player takes — selecting an answer, bumping into another player, scoring a point — needs an immediate, satisfying sound response. The audio feedback loop is tight: action → sound → emotional response → next action.

We use layered music systems that increase in intensity as rounds progress. Percussion layers add on, harmonic elements become more complex, the tempo subtly increases. Players don't consciously notice, but they feel the escalation.

### Slice It! (Rhythm Game)

A rhythm game where the audio feels wrong is a broken rhythm game. For Slice It!, audio timing precision is critical. We use the Web Audio API's high-resolution scheduling (`AudioContext.currentTime`, which runs on a separate high-priority thread) rather than `setTimeout` or `requestAnimationFrame`, which are subject to main-thread jank.

The input-to-audio latency budget for a rhythm game is around **20 milliseconds** — anything more and players perceive a desync between their actions and the audio response. The Web Audio API can meet this budget, but only if you schedule sounds ahead of time rather than triggering them reactively.

### Echoes of the Spire (Narrative Extraction Thriller)

Echoes uses audio for **atmosphere and tension**. Ambient soundscapes — dripping water, distant machinery, resonant hums — create the sense of being deep inside a structure. We use convolutional reverb (the Web Audio API's `ConvolverNode`) to simulate acoustic spaces, and we crossfade between ambient layers as the player moves between areas.

Music in Echoes is sparse and deliberate. Long stretches of gameplay have no music at all — just ambiance. When music does appear, it signals something important: a story beat, a danger, a revelation. This makes the music more impactful because it's not competing with a constant soundtrack.

### Temple of Joy (Philosophical Idle Clicker)

Idle games present an interesting audio challenge: the player might have the game running for hours. Audio that's engaging in the first minute becomes torture by the thirtieth. We use generative ambient music — evolving, non-repeating soundscapes built from randomized musical fragments — so that the audio environment stays fresh without requiring an hours-long composed soundtrack.

### Neon Driftway (Endless Racer)

Speed needs sound. In Neon Driftway, the audio design reinforces the sensation of velocity: engine frequencies pitch up with speed, the wind layer intensifies, the music becomes more driving. We use the Web Audio API's `BiquadFilterNode` to apply high-pass filtering that increases with speed, simulating the way wind noise masks lower frequencies at high velocity.

## Principles We Follow

Across all our games, we hold to a few core principles:

- **Audio is not optional.** We design and budget for sound from the beginning of development, not as a polish pass at the end. Sound is not decoration — it's structure.
- **Silence is a tool.** The absence of sound is as powerful as its presence. We deliberately create quiet moments to make loud moments hit harder.
- **Variation prevents fatigue.** Any sound that plays frequently (footsteps, UI clicks, coin pickups) has multiple variations with randomized pitch and volume. The human ear is incredibly sensitive to repetition, and even subtle variation makes sounds feel alive.
- **Respect the player's environment.** Browser games are played in all kinds of contexts — on a bus, in a library, at 2am with a sleeping partner nearby. We always provide easy-to-find volume controls and mute toggles, and we never punish players for playing with audio off.

## The Road Ahead

We're actively experimenting with **AudioWorklets** for custom DSP processing — think real-time audio effects that respond to game state, procedural sound synthesis that generates sounds from parameters rather than samples, and low-latency audio processing for our rhythm game pipeline.

We're also watching the development of the **WebCodecs API**, which promises more efficient audio decoding and could help with the codec limitations we currently work around.

Sound design in browser games is an evolving craft. The tools are getting better, the platform is getting more capable, and the creative possibilities are expanding. We're committed to making games that sound as thoughtfully designed as they look and play — because game feel isn't just visual. It's sonic.

title: Designing Synapse Storm for Cognitive Overload
description: The psychology of multitasking, the art of micro-challenges, and how Synapse Storm creates flow through chaos.
content:
## Your Brain Wasn't Built for This

Here's a fact that should humble every multitasker: **the human brain cannot truly multitask**. What we call multitasking is actually rapid task-switching — the brain toggling between activities, losing a small amount of efficiency with each switch. Research published by the American Psychological Association has consistently shown that this switching cost is real and measurable. A 2001 study by Joshua Rubinstein, David Meyer, and Jeffrey Evans, published in the *Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance*, found that task-switching can cost as much as 40% of productive time, with costs increasing as tasks become more complex.

Synapse Storm is a game designed around this cognitive reality. It's a **micro-challenge juggler** — a game that throws multiple small tasks at you simultaneously and asks you to handle all of them. Match patterns. Solve arithmetic. Track moving objects. Remember sequences. All at once, all in real time, all accelerating.

It's designed to overwhelm you. And then it's designed to help you find **flow** within that overwhelm.

## The WarioWare Inspiration

The micro-challenge format has a legendary ancestor: **WarioWare** (Nintendo, 2003). Yoshio Sakamoto's team created something revolutionary — a game composed entirely of "microgames" lasting 3-5 seconds each. Pick a nose. Dodge a car. Catch a falling object. Each microgame was trivially simple in isolation, but the rapid succession and escalating speed created an experience that was thrilling and hilarious.

WarioWare proved that **context-switching itself can be gameplay**. The challenge wasn't any individual task — it was the mental gear-shifting between tasks. Your brain had to load new rules every few seconds, and the comedy came from the gap between understanding and execution.

Synapse Storm takes this principle and pushes it in a different direction. Where WarioWare presents microgames **sequentially** (one at a time, very fast), Synapse Storm presents micro-challenges **simultaneously** (multiple at once, overlapping). This changes the cognitive demand from rapid sequential switching to genuine divided attention.

## Baba Is You and Rule Comprehension

Another unlikely influence on Synapse Storm is **Baba Is You** (Hempuli, 2019), the puzzle game where the rules of the game are physical objects you can manipulate. Baba Is You taught us something about cognitive load: **understanding the rules IS the gameplay**.

In Synapse Storm, each micro-challenge has its own rules. One challenge might ask you to tap the largest number. Another asks you to swipe in the direction of an arrow. A third asks you to tap objects of a specific color. Individually, these rules are trivial. But when three challenges are active simultaneously, the player must hold three rule sets in working memory while executing all of them.

This is directly related to what psychologists call **working memory capacity**. George Miller's classic 1956 paper, "The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two," established that humans can hold approximately 7 (±2) items in working memory. More recent research, including work by Nelson Cowan published in *Behavioral and Brain Sciences* (2001), suggests the true limit is closer to 4 items for most people.
